5 Sec. 7. Except as otherwise provided, IC 4-21.5-3 governs all
6 agency action taken by the department under this chapter or
7 IC 37-2-4-22 through IC 37-2-4-39. All proceedings for
8 administrative review under IC 4-21.5-3 or judicial review under
9 IC 4-21.5-5 shall be held in Marion County. The provisions of
10 IC 4-22-2 prescribing procedures for the adoption of rules by
11 agencies apply to the adoption of rules by the department of
12 financial institutions under this article.
13 Sec. 8. It is a violation of this article for a person or individual
14 subject to this article to:
15 (1) directly or indirectly employ any scheme, device, or
16 artifice to defraud or mislead borrowers or lenders or to
17 defraud any person;
18 (2) engage in any unfair or deceptive practice toward any
19 person;
20 (3) obtain property by fraud or misrepresentation;
21 (4) solicit or enter into a contract with a borrower that
22 provides in substance that the person or individual subject to
23 this article may earn a fee or commission through "best
24 efforts" to obtain a loan even though no loan is actually
25 obtained for the borrower;
26 (5) solicit, advertise, or enter into a contract for specific
27 interest rates, points, or other financing terms unless the
28 terms are actually available at the time of soliciting,
29 advertising, or contracting;
30 (6) conduct any business covered by this article without
31 holding a valid license as required under this article, or assist
32 or aid and abet any person in the conduct of business under
33 this article without a valid license as required under this
34 article;
35 (7) fail to make disclosures as required by this article and any
36 other applicable state or federal law, including regulations
37 under that law;
38 (8) fail to comply with this article or rules adopted under this
39 article, or fail to comply with any other state or federal law,
40 rule, or regulation, applicable to any business authorized or
41 conducted under this article;
42 (9) make, in any manner, any false or deceptive statement or
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92
254
1 representation, including, with regard to the rates, points, or
2 other financing terms or conditions for a mortgage
3 transaction, or engage in bait and switch advertising;
4 (10) negligently make any false statement or knowingly and
5 willfully make any omission of material fact in connection
6 with any information or reports filed with a governmental
7 agency or the NMLSR or in connection with any investigation
8 conducted by the director or another governmental agency;
9 (11) make any payment, threat, or promise, directly or
10 indirectly, to any person for the purposes of influencing the
11 independent judgment of the person in connection with a
12 mortgage transaction, or make any payment, threat, or
13 promise, directly or indirectly, to any appraiser of a property,
14 for the purposes of influencing the independent judgment of
15 the appraiser with respect to the value of the property;
16 (12) collect, charge, attempt to collect or charge, or use or
17 propose any agreement purporting to collect or charge any
18 fee prohibited by this article;
19 (13) cause or require a borrower to obtain property insurance
20 coverage in an amount that exceeds the replacement cost of
21 the improvements as established by the property insurer;
22 (14) fail to account truthfully for money belonging to a party
23 to a mortgage transaction; or
24 (15) knowingly withhold, abstract, remove, mutilate, destroy,
25 or secrete any books, records, computer records, or other
26 information subject to examination under this article.
27 Sec. 9. (a) After notice and an opportunity to be heard, the
28 department may order a creditor, or a person acting on behalf of
29 the creditor, to cease and desist from engaging in violations of this
30 article. A respondent aggrieved by an order of the department may
31 obtain judicial review of the order and the department may obtain
32 an order of the court for enforcement of its order in any civil court.
33 The proceeding for review or enforcement is initiated by filing a
34 petition in the court. Copies of the petition shall be served upon all
35 parties of record.
36 (b) Within thirty (30) days after service of the petition for
37 review upon the department, or within any further time the court
38 may allow, the department shall transmit to the court the original
39 or a certified copy of the entire record upon which the order is
40 based, including any transcript of testimony, which need not be
41 printed. By stipulation of all parties to the review proceeding, the
42 record may be shortened. After hearing the court may:
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92
255
1 (1) reverse or modify the order if the findings of fact of the
2 department are clearly erroneous in view of the reliable,
3 probative, and substantial evidence on the whole record;
4 (2) grant any temporary relief or restraining order it deems
5 just; and
6 (3) enter an order enforcing, modifying, and enforcing as
7 modified, or setting aside in whole or in part the order of the
8 department, or remanding the case to the department for
9 further proceedings.
10 (c) An objection not urged at the hearing shall not be considered
11 by the court unless the failure to urge the objection is excused for
12 good cause shown. A party may move the court to remand the case
13 to the department in the interest of justice for the purpose of
14 adducing additional specified and material evidence and seeking
15 finding thereon upon good cause shown for the failure to adduce
16 this evidence before the department.
17 (d) The jurisdiction of the court shall be exclusive and its final
18 judgment or decree shall be subject to review by the court on
19 appeal in the same manner and form and with the same effect as in
20 appeals from a final judgment or decree. The department's copy of
21 the testimony shall be available at reasonable times to all parties
22 for examination without cost.
23 (e) A proceeding for review under this section must be initiated
24 within thirty (30) days after a copy of the order of the department
25 is received. If no proceeding is so initiated, the department may
26 obtain a decree of the civil court for enforcement of its order upon
27 a showing that an order was issued in compliance with this section,
28 that no proceeding for review was initiated within thirty (30) days
29 after copy of the order was received, and that the respondent is
30 subject to the jurisdiction of the court.
31 (f) With respect to unconscionable agreements or fraudulent or
32 unconscionable conduct by the respondent, the department may
33 not issue an order pursuant to this section but may bring a civil
34 action for an injunction under section 12 of this chapter.
35 Sec. 10. If it is claimed that a person has engaged in conduct
36 subject to an order by the department under section 9 of this
37 chapter or by a court under sections 11 through 13 of this chapter,
38 the department may accept an assurance in writing that the person
39 will not engage in the conduct in the future. If a person giving an
40 assurance of discontinuance fails to comply with its terms, the
41 assurance is evidence that prior to the assurance the person
42 engaged in the conduct described in the assurance.
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92
256
1 Sec. 11. The department may bring a civil action to restrain a
2 person from violating this article or another state or federal law or
3 regulation, and for other appropriate relief.
4 Sec. 12. (a) The department may bring a civil action to restrain
5 a creditor or a person acting in behalf of a creditor from engaging
6 in a course of:
7 (1) making or enforcing unconscionable terms or provisions
8 of consumer credit sales, consumer leases, or consumer loans;
9 (2) fraudulent or unconscionable conduct in inducing debtors
10 to enter into consumer credit sales, consumer leases, or
11 consumer loans; or
12 (3) fraudulent or unconscionable conduct in the collection of
13 debts arising from consumer credit sales, consumer leases, or
14 consumer loans.
15 (b) In an action brought pursuant to this section the court may
16 grant relief only if it finds:
17 (1) that the respondent has made unconscionable agreements
18 or has engaged or is likely to engage in a course of fraudulent
19 or unconscionable conduct;
20 (2) that the agreements or conduct of the respondent has
21 caused or is likely to cause injury to consumers; and
22 (3) that the respondent has been able to cause or will be able
23 to cause the injury primarily because the transactions
24 involved are credit transactions.
25 (c) In applying this section, consideration shall be given to each
26 of the following factors, among others:
27 (1) belief by the creditor at the time consumer credit sales,
28 consumer leases, or consumer loans are made that there was
29 no reasonable probability of payment in full of the obligation
30 by the debtor;
31 (2) in the case of consumer credit sales or consumer leases,
32 knowledge by the seller or lessor at the time of the sale or
33 lease of the inability of the buyer or lessee to receive
34 substantial benefits from the property or services sold or
35 leased;
36 (3) in the case of consumer credit sales or consumer leases,
37 gross disparity between the price of the property or services
38 sold or leased and the value of the property or services
39 measured by the price at which similar property or services
40 are readily obtainable in credit transactions by like buyers or
41 lessees;
42 (4) the fact that the creditor contracted for or received
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92
257
1 separate charges for insurance with respect to consumer
2 credit sales or consumer loans with the effect of making the
3 sales or loans, considered as a whole, unconscionable; and
4 (5) the fact that the respondent has knowingly taken
5 advantage of the inability of the debtor reasonably to protect
6 the debtor's interests by reason of physical or mental
7 infirmities, ignorance, illiteracy or inability to understand the
8 language of the agreement, or similar factors.
9 (d) In an action brought pursuant to this section, a charge or
10 practice expressly permitted by this article is not in itself
11 unconscionable.
12 Sec. 13. With respect to an action brought to enjoin violations
13 of this article under section 11 of this chapter or unconscionable
14 agreements or fraudulent or unconscionable conduct under section
15 12 of this chapter, the department may apply to the court for
16 appropriate temporary relief against a respondent, pending final
17 determination of proceedings. If the court finds after a hearing
18 held upon notice to the respondent that there is reasonable cause
19 to believe that the respondent is engaging in or is likely to engage
20 in conduct sought to be restrained, it may grant any temporary
21 relief or restraining order it deems appropriate.
22 Sec. 14. (a) After demand, the department may bring a civil
23 action against a creditor for making or collecting charges in excess
24 of those permitted by this article. An action may relate to
25 transactions with more than one debtor. If it is found that an excess
26 charge has been made, the court shall order the respondent to
27 refund to the debtor or debtors the amount of the excess charge. If
28 a creditor has made an excess charge in deliberate violation of or
29 in reckless disregard for this article, or if a creditor has refused to
30 refund an excess charge within a reasonable time after demand by
31 the debtor or the department, the court may also order the
32 respondent to pay to the debtor or debtors a civil penalty in an
33 amount determined by the court not in excess of the greater of
34 either the amount of the credit service or loan finance charge or
35 ten (10) times the amount of the charge. Refunds and penalties to
36 which the debtor is entitled pursuant to this subsection may be set
37 off against the debtor's obligation. If a debtor brings an action
38 against a creditor to recover an excess charge or civil penalty, an
39 action by the department to recover for the same excess charge or
40 civil penalty shall be stayed while the debtor's action is pending
41 and shall be dismissed if the debtor's action is dismissed with
42 prejudice or results in a final judgment granting or denying the
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92
258
1 debtor's claim. With respect to excess charges arising from sales
2 made pursuant to revolving charge accounts or from loans made
3 pursuant to revolving loan accounts, no action pursuant to this
4 subsection may be brought more than two (2) years after the time
5 the excess charge was made. With respect to excess charges arising
6 from other consumer credit sales or consumer loans, no action
7 pursuant to this subsection may be brought more than one (1) year
8 after the due date of the last scheduled payment of the agreement
9 pursuant to which the charge was made. If the creditor establishes
10 by a preponderance of evidence that a violation is unintentional or
11 the result of a bona fide error, no liability to pay a penalty shall be
12 imposed under this subsection.
13 (b) The department may bring a civil action against a creditor
14 or a person acting in the creditor's behalf to recover a civil penalty
15 for willfully violating this article, and if the court finds that the
16 defendant has engaged in a course of repeated and willful
17 violations of this article, it may assess a civil penalty of no more
18 than five thousand dollars ($5,000). No civil penalty pursuant to
19 this subsection may be imposed for violations of this article
20 occurring more than two (2) years before the action is brought or
21 for making unconscionable agreements or engaging in a course of
22 fraudulent or unconscionable conduct.
23 (c) If the department determines, after notice and opportunity
24 for the person to be heard, that a person has violated this article,
25 the department may, in addition to or instead of all other remedies
26 available under this section, impose upon the person a civil penalty
27 not greater than ten thousand dollars ($10,000) per violation.
28 Sec. 15. The grant of powers to the department in this article
29 does not affect remedies available to debtors under this article or
30 under other principles of law or equity.
31 Sec. 16. The department may bring actions or proceedings in a
32 court in a county in which an act on which the action or proceeding
33 is based occurred or in a county in which respondent resides or
34 transacts business or in a county otherwise authorized by rule or
35 venue laws.
36 Sec. 17. (a) Subject to subsection (b), if the director determines
37 that a director, an officer, or a manager of a creditor:
38 (1) has committed a violation of a statute, a rule, a final cease
39 and desist order, a condition imposed in writing by the
40 director in connection with the grant of an application or
41 other request by the creditor, or a written agreement between
42 the creditor and the director or the department;
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92
259
1 (2) has committed fraudulent or unconscionable conduct; or
2 (3) has been convicted of a felony under the laws of Indiana or
3 any other jurisdiction;
4 the director may issue and serve upon the person a notice of
5 charges and of the director's intent to issue an order removing the
6 person from the person's office or employment, an order
7 prohibiting participation by the person in the conduct of the affairs
8 of any creditor, or an order both removing the person and
9 prohibiting the person's participation.
10 (b) A violation, practice, or breach described in subsection (a)
11 is subject to the authority of the director under subsections (a) and
12 (c) if the director finds any of the following:
13 (1) The interests of the creditor's customers could be seriously
14 prejudiced by reason of the violation, practice, or breach.
15 (2) The violation, practice, or breach involves an act of fraud,
16 dishonesty, theft, breach of trust, money laundering, or
17 wrongful taking of property on the part of the officer,
18 director, or manager involved.
19 (3) The violation, practice, or breach demonstrates a willful
20 or continuing disregard by the officer, director, or manager
21 for state or federal law and regulations, and for the consumer
22 protections contained in this article.
23 (c) Subject to subsections (a) and (b), a person who has been
24 convicted of a felony under the laws of Indiana or any other
25 jurisdiction may not serve as an officer, a director, or a manager
26 of a creditor, or serve in any similar capacity, unless the person
27 obtains the written consent of the director.
28 (d) A creditor that willfully permits a person to serve the
29 creditor in violation of subsection (c) is subject to a civil penalty of
30 five hundred dollars ($500) for each day the violation occurs.
31 (e) A creditor shall give the department written notice of the
32 resignation, discharge, or termination of an employee, independent
33 contractor, or agent against whom allegations were made that
34 accused the employee, independent contractor, or agent of:
35 (1) violating this article or other laws, regulations, rules, or
36 industry standards of conduct applicable to consumer credit
37 transactions; or
38 (2) fraud, dishonesty, theft, breach of trust, money
39 laundering, or the wrongful taking of property.
40 The creditor shall provide the department the notice required
41 under this subsection not later than thirty (30) days after the
42 effective date of the resignation, discharge, or termination.
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92
260
1 Sec. 18. (a) A notice issued under section 17 of this chapter
2 must:
3 (1) be in writing;
4 (2) contain a statement of:
5 (A) the facts constituting the alleged violation, practice, or
6 breach;
7 (B) the facts alleged in support of the violation, practice, or
8 breach; and
9 (C) the director's intention to issue an order under section
10 20(a) of this chapter;
11 (3) be delivered to the board of directors of the creditor;
12 (4) be delivered to the officer, director, or manager to which
13 the notice applies;
14 (5) specify the procedures that must be followed to initiate a
15 hearing to contest the alleged violation, practice, or breach;
16 and
17 (6) if the director suspends or prohibits the officer, director,
18 or manager from participation in the affairs of the creditor as
19 described under subsection (e), contain a statement of the
20 suspension or prohibition.
21 (b) If a hearing is requested not later than ten (10) days after
22 service of the notice described under subsection (a), the
23 department shall hold a hearing concerning the alleged violation,
24 practice, or breach. The hearing shall be held not later than
25 forty-five (45) days after receipt of the request. The department,
26 based on the evidence presented at the hearing, shall enter a final
27 order in accordance with section 20 of this chapter.
28 (c) If no hearing is requested within the period of time specified
29 in subsection (b), the director may proceed to issue a final order
30 under section 20 of this chapter on the basis of the facts set forth in
31 the notice described under subsection (a).
32 (d) An officer, a director, or a manager of a creditor who is
33 removed from a position under a removal order under section 20
34 of this chapter that has become final may not, without the approval
35 of the director, participate in the conduct of the affairs of a licensee
36 described under IC 37-2-4.
37 (e) The director may, for the protection of the creditor or the
38 interests of the creditor's customers, suspend from office or
39 prohibit from participation in the affairs of the creditor an officer,
40 a director, or a manager of a creditor who is the subject of a
41 written notice served by the director under section 17(a) of this
42 chapter. A suspension or prohibition under this subsection becomes
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92
261
1 effective upon service of the notice under section 17(a) of this
2 chapter. Unless stayed by a court in a proceeding authorized by
3 subsection (f), the suspension or prohibition remains in effect
4 pending completion of the proceedings related to the notice served
5 under section 17(a) of this chapter and until the effective date of an
6 order entered by the department under subsection (b) or the
7 director under subsection (c). If the director suspends or prohibits
8 participation of an officer, a director, or a manager under this
9 subsection, copies of the notice shall also be served upon the
10 creditor or affiliate of which the person is an officer, a director, or
11 a manager.
12 (f) Not more than fifteen (15) days after an officer, a director, or
13 a manager has been suspended from office or prohibited from
14 participation in the conduct of the affairs of the creditor or affiliate
15 under subsection (e), the officer, director, or manager may apply
16 to a court having jurisdiction for a stay of the suspension or
17 prohibition pending completion of the proceedings related to the
18 notice served under section 17(a) of this chapter. The court may
19 stay a suspension of prohibition of the officer, director, or
20 manager.
21 (g) The department shall maintain an official record of a
22 proceeding under this chapter.
23 Sec. 19. If the director enters into a consent to a final order with
24 a director, an officer, or a manager, the director is not required to
25 issue and serve a notice of charges upon the director, officer, or
26 manager under section 17 of this chapter. A consent agreement
27 may be negotiated and entered into before or after the issuance of
28 a notice of charges. The director shall provide a copy of the consent
29 order to the board of directors of the creditor.
30 Sec. 20. (a) Subject to section 18 of this chapter, if, after a
31 hearing described in section 18(b) of this chapter, the department
32 determines that a director, an officer, or a manager of a creditor
33 has committed an act described in section 17 of this chapter, the
34 department may issue a final order. If a hearing is not requested
35 within the time specified in section 18(b) of this chapter, the
36 director may issue a final order on the basis of the facts set forth
37 in the written notice served under section 18(a) of this chapter.
38 (b) Unless the director has entered into a consent agreement
39 described in section 19 of this chapter, a final order must include
40 separately stated findings of fact and conclusions of law for all
41 aspects of the order.
42 (c) In a final order under this section, the department or the
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92
262
1 director, as appropriate, may order one (1) or more of the
2 following with respect to an officer, a director, or a manager of a
3 creditor:
4 (1) The removal of the officer, director, or manager from the
5 person's office, position, or employment.
6 (2) A prohibition against any participation by the officer,
7 director, or manager in the conduct of the affairs of any
8 creditor.
9 (3) If the subject of the order is an officer or a director of a
10 creditor, and subject to section 22 of this chapter, the
11 imposition of a civil penalty not to exceed fifteen thousand
12 dollars ($15,000) for each practice, violation, or act that:
13 (A) is described in section 17 of this chapter; and
14 (B) is found to exist by the department or the director.
15 (d) A final order shall be issued in writing not later than ninety
16 (90) days after conclusion of a hearing held under section 18(b) of
17 this chapter, unless this period is waived or extended with the
18 written consent of all parties or for good cause shown.
19 (e) If the officer, director, or manager does not appear
20 individually or by an authorized representative at a hearing held
21 under section 18(b) of this chapter, the officer, director, or
22 manager is considered to have consented to the issuance of a final
23 order.
24 (f) The director may keep a final order confidential if the
25 director determines that the immediate release of the order would
26 endanger the stability of the creditor. However, after two (2) years
27 following the date that an order is issued, a final order is no longer
28 confidential.
29 (g) The remedies provided in this chapter are in addition to
30 other remedies contained in this article.
31 Sec. 21. (a) A final order issued under section 20 of this chapter
32 is effective the eleventh day after the date the order is served.
33 However, a final order issued upon consent under section 19 of this
34 chapter is effective at the time specified in the order.
35 (b) A final order remains effective and enforceable as provided
36 in the order.
37 (c) The department or a reviewing court may stay, modify, or
38 vacate a final order.
39 Sec. 22. (a) The director or the department, as appropriate, shall
40 consider the following factors in determining the amount of a civil
41 penalty that should be assessed against a director or an officer
42 under section 20(c)(3) of this chapter:
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92
263
1 (1) The appropriateness of the civil penalty with respect to the
2 financial resources and good faith of the individual charged.
3 (2) The gravity of the practice, violation, or breach.
4 (3) The history of previous practices, violations, or breaches.
5 (4) The economic benefit derived by the individual from the
6 practice, violation, or breach.
7 (5) Other factors that justice requires.
8 (b) A creditor may not indemnify a director or an officer for a
9 civil penalty imposed against the director or officer under this
10 section.
11 (c) Civil penalties shall be deposited in the financial institutions
12 fund established by IC 28-11-2-9.
13 Sec. 23. The director may enforce any of the following by
14 applying for appropriate relief to a court having jurisdiction:
15 (1) An order issued under section 19 or 20 of this chapter.
16 (2) A written agreement entered into by the director or the
17 department and a director, an officer, or a manager of a
18 creditor.
19 (3) Any condition imposed in writing by the director or the
20 department on a director, an officer, or a manager of a
21 creditor.
22 Sec. 24. (a) This section and sections 25 and 26 of this chapter
23 apply to a person, including a depository institution, but not
24 including a collection agency licensed under IC 25-11-1, engaged
25 in Indiana in any of the following:
26 (1) Making consumer credit sales, consumer leases, or
27 consumer loans.
28 (2) Taking assignments of rights against debtors that arise
29 from sales, leases, or loans by a person having an office or a
30 place of business in Indiana.
31 (3) Undertaking direct collection of payments from the
32 debtors or enforcement of rights against the debtors.
33 (4) Placing consumer credit insurance, receiving commissions
34 for consumer credit insurance, or acting as a limited line
35 credit insurance producer in the sale of consumer credit
36 insurance.
37 (5) Selling insurance or other benefits, the charges for which
38 are approved by the department as additional charges under
39 IC 37-2-3-5 or IC 37-2-4-5.
40 (b) This section and sections 25 and 26 of this chapter are not
41 applicable to a seller whose credit sales consist entirely of sales
42 made pursuant to a seller credit card issued by a person other than
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

1 the seller if the issuer of the card has complied with the provisions

3 (c) This section and sections 25 and 26 of this chapter apply to
4 a seller whose credit sales are made using credit cards that:
5 (1) are issued by a lender;
6 (2) are in the name of the seller; and
7 (3) can be used by the buyer or lessee only for purchases or

9 Sec. 25. (a) Persons that are subject to this section and sections
10 24 and 26 of this chapter shall file notification with the department
11 within thirty (30) days after commencing business in Indiana and
12 thereafter on an annual basis, on the date set forth in subsection
13 (b). The notification shall state the:
14 (1) name of the person;
15 (2) name in which business is transacted if different from
16 subdivision (1);
17 (3) address of principal office, which may be outside Indiana;
18 and
19 (4) address of all offices or retail stores, if any, in Indiana at
20 which consumer credit sales, consumer leases, or consumer
21 loans are made, or in the case of a person taking assignments
22 of obligations, the offices or places of business within Indiana
23 at which business is transacted.
24 (b) A person required to be licensed under this article shall file
25 the notification required by subsection (a) not later than December
26 31 of each year. All other persons subject to this section shall file
27 the notification required by subsection (a) not later than January
28 31 of each year.
29 (c) Persons subject to sections 24 and 26 of this chapter and this
30 section shall notify the department not later than thirty (30) days
31 after the person:
32 (1) has a change in name, address, or principals;
33 (2) opens a new branch, closes an existing branch, or relocates
34 an existing branch;
35 (3) files for bankruptcy or reorganization;
36 (4) is notified that the person is subject to revocation or
37 suspension proceedings by a state or governmental authority
38 with regard to the person's activities; or
39 (5) has been convicted of a felony involving fraud, deceit, or
40 misrepresentation under the laws of Indiana or any other
41 jurisdiction.
42 Sec. 26. (a) Persons required to file notification who are sellers,
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92
265
1 lessors, or lenders shall pay a fee in an amount and at intervals to
2 be prescribed by the director under IC 28-11-3-5. The fee shall be
3 a set amount for each one hundred thousand dollars ($100,000), or
4 part thereof, in excess of one hundred thousand dollars ($100,000),
5 of the original unpaid balances arising from consumer credit sales,
6 consumer leases, and consumer loans made in Indiana and held
7 either by the seller, lessor, or lender for more than thirty (30) days
8 after the inception of the sale, lease, or loan giving rise to the
9 obligations, or by an assignee who has not filed notification. A
10 refinancing of a sale, lease, or loan resulting in an increase in the
11 amount of an obligation is a new sale, lease, or loan to the extent of
12 the increase. In prescribing the fee, the department shall consider
13 the costs and expense incurred or estimated to be incurred by the
14 department in the administration of this article, including, but not
15 limited to, the supervision, regulation, and examination of persons
16 subject to the provisions of the article.
17 (b) Persons required to file notification who are assignees shall
18 pay a fee as prescribed and fixed by the department under
19 subsection (a) on the unpaid balances at the time of the assignment
20 of obligations arising from consumer credit sales, consumer leases,
21 and consumer loans made in Indiana taken by assignment during
22 the preceding calendar year, but an assignee need not pay a fee
23 with respect to an obligation on which the assignor or other person
24 has already paid a fee.
25 (c) Persons required to file notification who are assignors shall
26 pay a fee as prescribed by the department under subsection (a) on
27 the unpaid balances at the time of the assignment of obligations
28 arising from consumer credit sales, consumer leases, and consumer
29 loans made in Indiana during the preceding calendar year unless
30 the assignee has already paid the fees.
31 (d) Persons required to renew a license under IC 37-2-4-31 may
32 deduct the fees paid under IC 37-2-4-26(h)(2) and
33 IC 37-2-4-26(h)(3), as applicable, from fees paid under this section.
34 (e) A person that is required to file notification under section 25
35 of this chapter shall pay a fee at the same rate as prescribed and
36 fixed by the department under subsection (a) on the original
37 unpaid balances of all closed end credit obligations originating
38 from the person's place of business during the time preceding the
39 notification as specified under subsection (a), unless the fees for the
40 obligations have been paid by another person.

2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92
266
1 ARTICLE 3. SMALL LOANS
2 Chapter 1. General Provisions
3 Sec. 1. (a) Except as otherwise provided, all provisions of
4 IC 37-2 applying to consumer loans, including IC 37-2-4-25, apply
5 to small loans.
6 (b) Subject to section 4 of this chapter, a person may not
7 regularly engage in Indiana in any of the following actions unless
8 the department first issues to the person a license under this
9 article:
10 (1) The making of small loans.
11 (2) Taking assignments of small loans.
12 (3) Undertaking the direct collection of payments from or the
13 enforcement of rights against debtors arising from small
14 loans.
15 (c) Subject to section 4 of this chapter, a person that seeks
16 licensure under this article:
17 (1) shall apply to the department for a license in the form and
18 manner prescribed by the department; and
19 (2) is subject to the same licensure requirements and
20 procedures as an applicant for a license to make consumer
21 loans (other than mortgage transactions) under IC 37-2-4-23.
22 (d) A person that seeks to make, take assignments of, or
23 undertake the direct collection of payments from or the
24 enforcement of rights against debtors arising from both:
25 (1) small loans under this chapter; and
26 (2) consumer loans (other than mortgage transactions) that
27 are not small loans;
28 must obtain a separate license from the department for each type
29 of loan, as described in IC 37-2-4-23(e).
30 Sec. 2. This article applies to:
31 (1) a lender;
32 (2) a bank, savings association, credit union, or other state or
33 federally regulated financial institution except those that are
34 specifically exempt regarding limitations on interest rates and
35 fees; or
36 (3) a person, if the department determines that a transaction
37 is:
38 (A) in substance a disguised loan; or
39 (B) the application of subterfuge for the purpose of
40 avoiding this chapter.
41 Sec. 3. A loan that:
42 (1) does not qualify as a small loan under IC 37-3-2-10;
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92
267
1 (2) is for a term shorter than that specified in IC 37-3-3-5(a);
2 or
3 (3) is made in violation of section IC 37-3-3-2, IC 37-3-3-5,
4 IC 37-3-3-6, IC 37-3-3-8, or IC 37-3-3-12;
5 is subject to this article. The department may conform the loan
6 finance charge for a loan described in this section to the limitations
7 set forth in IC 37-2-4-35(b).
8 Sec. 4. Notwithstanding IC 37-2-2, for purposes of section 1(b)
9 of this chapter, a person "regularly engages" in any of the
10 activities described in section 1(b) of this chapter with respect to a
11 small loan if the person:
12 (1) performed any of the activities described in section 1(b) of
13 this chapter with respect to a small loan at least one (1) time
14 in the preceding calendar year; or
15 (2) performs or will perform any of the activities described in
16 section 1(b) of this chapter with respect to a small loan at least
17 one (1) time in the current calendar year if the person did not
18 perform any of the activities described in section 1(b) of this
19 chapter with respect to a small loan at least one (1) time in the
20 preceding calendar year.
21 Chapter 2. Definitions
22 Sec. 1. Except as otherwise provided, the definitions set forth in
23 IC 37-2-2 apply throughout this article.
24 Sec. 2. The definitions set forth in this chapter apply throughout
25 this article.
26 Sec. 3. "Check" has the meaning set forth in IC 26-1-3.1-104.
27 Sec. 4. "Consecutive small loan" means a new small loan
28 agreement that the lender enters with the same borrower not later
29 than seven (7) calendar days after a previous small loan made to
30 that borrower is paid in full.
31 Sec. 5. "Lender" means a person that acquires and retains a
32 license issued by the department of financial institutions under this
33 chapter to engage in small loans.
34 Sec. 6. "Monthly gross income" means the income received by
35 the borrower in the thirty (30) day period preceding the
36 borrower's application for a small loan under this chapter and
37 exclusive of any income other than regular gross pay received, or
38 as otherwise determined by the department.
39 Sec. 7. "Paid in full" means the termination of a small loan
40 through:
41 (1) the presentment of the borrower's check for payment by
42 the drawee bank or the exercise by the lender of an
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92
268
1 authorization to debit an account of the borrower; or
2 (2) the return of a check to a borrower who redeems it for
3 consideration.
4 Sec. 8. "Principal" means the total of:
5 (1) the net amount paid to, receivable by, or paid or payable
6 from the account of the borrower; and
7 (2) to the extent that the payment is deferred, the additional
8 charges permitted by this chapter that are not included in
9 subdivision (1).
10 Sec. 9. "Renewal" refers to a small loan that takes the place of
11 an existing small loan by:
12 (1) renewing;
13 (2) repaying;
14 (3) refinancing; or
15 (4) consolidating;
16 a small loan with the proceeds of another small loan made to the
17 same borrower by a lender.
18 Sec. 10. (a) "Small loan" means a loan:
19 (1) with a principal loan amount that is at least fifty dollars
20 ($50) and not more than five hundred fifty dollars ($550); and
21 (2) in which the lender holds the borrower's check for a
22 specific period, or receives the borrower's written
23 authorization to debit the borrower's account (other than as
24 a result of default) under an agreement, either express or
25 implied, for a specific period, before the lender:
26 (A) offers the check for deposit or presentment; or
27 (B) exercises the authorization to debit the borrower's
28 account.
29 (b) The amount of five hundred fifty dollars ($550) in subsection
30 (a)(1) is subject to change under the provisions on adjustment of
31 dollar amounts set forth in IC 37-2-1-5. However, notwithstanding
32 IC 37-2-1-5(a), the Reference Base Index to be used under this
33 subsection is the Index for October 2006.
34 Chapter 3. Regulation of Small Loans
35 Sec. 1. A lender is not considered a financial institution, except
36 for purposes of IC 28-1.
37 Sec. 2. (a) Finance charges on the first two hundred fifty dollars
38 ($250) of a small loan are limited to fifteen percent (15%) of the
39 principal.
40 (b) Finance charges on the amount of a small loan greater than
41 two hundred fifty dollars ($250) and less than or equal to four
42 hundred dollars ($400) are limited to thirteen percent (13%) of the
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92
269
1 amount over two hundred fifty dollars ($250) and less than or
2 equal to four hundred dollars ($400).
3 (c) Finance charges on the amount of the small loan greater
4 than four hundred dollars ($400) and less than or equal to five
5 hundred fifty dollars ($550) are limited to ten percent (10%) of the
6 amount over four hundred dollars ($400) and less than or equal to
7 five hundred fifty dollars ($550).
8 (d) The amount of five hundred fifty dollars ($550) in subsection
9 (c) is subject to change under the provisions on adjustment of
10 dollar amounts set forth in IC 37-2-1-5. However, notwithstanding
11 IC 37-2-1-5(a), the Reference Base Index to be used under this
12 subsection is the Index for October 2006.
13 Sec. 3. (a) Notwithstanding any other law, the only fee that may
14 be contracted for and received by the lender or an assignee on a
15 small loan is a charge, not to exceed twenty-five dollars ($25), for
16 each:
17 (1) return by a bank or other depository institution of a
18 dishonored:
19 (A) check;
20 (B) electronic funds transfer;
21 (C) negotiable order of withdrawal; or
22 (D) share draft;
23 issued by the borrower; or
24 (2) time an authorization to debit the borrower's account is
25 dishonored.
26 This additional charge may be assessed one (1) time regardless of
27 how many times a check or an authorization to debit the
28 borrower's account may be submitted by the lender and
29 dishonored.
30 (b) A lender may:
31 (1) present a borrower's check for payment; or
32 (2) exercise a borrower's authorization to debit the
33 borrower's account;
34 not more than three (3) times.
35 Sec. 4. (a) For purposes of this section, the lender shall disclose
36 to the borrower to whom credit is extended with respect to a small
37 loan the information required by the Consumer Credit Protection
38 Act (15 U.S.C. 1601 et seq.).
39 (b) In addition to the requirements of subsection (a), the lender
40 must conspicuously display in bold type a notice to the public both
41 in the lending area of each business location and in the loan
42 documents the following statement:
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92
270
1 "WARNING: A small loan is not intended to meet long term
2 financial needs. A small loan should be used only to meet
3 short term cash needs. The cost of your small loan may be
4 higher than loans offered by other lending institutions. Small
5 loans are regulated by the State of Indiana Department of
6 Financial Institutions.
7 A borrower may rescind a small loan without cost by paying
8 the cash amount of the principal of the small loan to the
9 lender not later than the end of the business day immediately
10 following the day on which the borrower receives the loan
11 proceeds.".
12 (c) The statement required in subsection (b) must be in:
13 (1) 14 point bold face type in the loan documents; and
14 (2) not less than one (1) inch bold print in the lending area of
15 the business location.
16 (d) When a borrower enters into a small loan, the lender shall
17 provide the borrower with a pamphlet approved by the
18 department that describes:
19 (1) the availability of debt management and credit counseling
20 services; and
21 (2) the borrower's rights and responsibilities in the
22 transaction.
23 Sec. 5. (a) A small loan may not be made for a term of less than
24 fourteen (14) days.
25 (b) If five (5) consecutive small loans have been made to a
26 borrower after the borrower's initial small loan, another small
27 loan may not be made to that borrower within seven (7) days after
28 the fifth consecutive small loan is paid in full. After the borrower's
29 fifth consecutive small loan, the balance must be paid in full.
30 (c) Subject to subsection (d), whenever a borrower has entered
31 into an initial small loan followed by three (3) consecutive small
32 loans, the lender shall offer the borrower the option to repay:
33 (1) the third consecutive small loan; and
34 (2) subject to subsection (b), any small loan entered into after
35 the third consecutive small loan;
36 under an extended payment plan. At the time of execution of a
37 small loan described in subdivision (1) or (2), the lender shall
38 disclose to the borrower the extended payment plan option by
39 providing the borrower a written description of the extended
40 payment plan option in a separate disclosure document approved
41 by the director.
42 (d) A lender shall offer an extended payment plan under
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92
271
1 subsection (c) under the following terms and conditions:
2 (1) A borrower shall be permitted to request an extended
3 payment plan at any time during the term of a third or
4 subsequent consecutive small loan if:
5 (A) the borrower has not defaulted on the outstanding
6 small loan; and
7 (B) the rescission period under section 6(f) of this chapter
8 has expired.
9 (2) An extended payment plan must allow the outstanding
10 small loan to be paid in at least four (4) equal installments
11 over a period of not less than sixty (60) days.
12 (3) An agreement for an extended payment plan may not
13 require a borrower to pay any amount before the original
14 maturity date of the outstanding small loan.
15 (4) The lender may not assess any fee or charge on a borrower
16 for entering into an extended payment plan.
17 (5) An agreement for an extended payment plan must be in
18 writing and acknowledged by both the borrower and the
19 lender.
20 (6) A borrower may not enter into another small loan
21 transaction while engaged in an extended payment plan.
22 (7) A lender shall not:
23 (A) compel, advise, solicit, or coerce a borrower to not
24 exercise the borrower's right to request an extended
25 payment plan;
26 (B) discourage a borrower from exercising the borrower's
27 right to request an extended payment plan; or
28 (C) take any other action to influence a borrower's right to
29 request an extended payment plan.
30 (e) An agreement for an extended payment plan under
31 subsection (c):
32 (1) shall be considered an extension of the outstanding small
33 loan; and
34 (2) may not be considered a new loan.
35 Sec. 6. (a) A lender is prohibited from making a small loan to a
36 borrower if the total of:
37 (1) the principal amount and finance charges of the small loan
38 to be issued; plus
39 (2) any other small loan balances that the borrower has
40 outstanding with any lender;
41 exceeds twenty percent (20%) of the borrower's monthly gross
42 income.
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92
272
1 (b) A small loan may be secured by only one (1) check or
2 authorization to debit the borrower's account per small loan. The
3 check or electronic debit may not exceed the amount advanced to
4 or on behalf of the borrower plus loan finance charges contracted
5 for and permitted.
6 (c) A borrower may make partial payments in any amount on
7 the small loan without charge at any time before the due date of the
8 small loan.
9 (d) After any payment is made on a small loan, whether the
10 payment is made in part or in full before, on, or after the due date
11 of the small loan, the lender shall give a signed and dated receipt
12 to the borrower making a payment showing the amount paid and
13 the balance due on the small loan.
14 (e) The lender shall provide to each borrower a copy of the
15 required loan documents before the disbursement of the loan
16 proceeds.
17 (f) A borrower may rescind a small loan without cost by paying
18 the cash amount of the principal of the small loan to the lender not
19 later than the end of the business day immediately following the
20 day on which the borrower receives the proceeds.
21 (g) A lender shall not enter into a renewal with a borrower. If
22 a loan is paid in full, a subsequent loan is not a renewal.
23 Sec. 7. A small loan may not be secured by personal property
24 other than a check or electronic debit.
25 Sec. 8. (a) As used in this section, "commercially reasonable
26 method of verification" means a private consumer credit reporting
27 service that the department determines to be capable of providing
28 a lender with adequate verification information necessary to
29 ensure compliance with subsection (d).
30 (b) With respect to a small loan, no lender may permit a person
31 to become obligated under more than one (1) loan agreement with
32 the lender at any time.
33 (c) A lender shall not make a small loan that, when combined
34 with the outstanding balance on another outstanding small loan
35 owed to another lender, exceeds a total of five hundred fifty dollars
36 ($550), excluding finance charges. A lender shall not make a small
37 loan to a borrower who has two (2) or more small loans
38 outstanding, regardless of the total value of the small loans. The
39 amount of five hundred fifty dollars ($550) in this subsection is
40 subject to change under the provisions on adjustment of dollar
41 amounts (IC 37-2-1-5). However, notwithstanding IC 37-2-1-5(a),
42 the Reference Base Index to be used under this subsection is the
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92
273
1 Index for October 2006.
2 (d) A lender complies with subsection (c) if the lender
3 independently verifies the total number of outstanding small loans
4 and the total outstanding balance of those small loans for a
5 customer through a commercially reasonable method of
6 verification. A lender's method of verifying whether a borrower
7 has any outstanding small loans and the total outstanding balance
8 of any loans will be considered commercially reasonable if the
9 method includes a manual investigation or an electronic query of:
10 (1) the lender's own records, including both records
11 maintained at the location where the borrower is applying for
12 the transaction and records maintained at other locations
13 within the state that are owned and operated by the lender;
14 and
15 (2) an available third party data base provided by a private
16 consumer reporting service, subject to the identification
17 verification requirements set forth in subsection (l).
18 (e) The department shall monitor the effectiveness of private
19 consumer credit reporting services in providing the verification
20 information required under subsection (d). If the department
21 determines that a commercially reasonable method of verification
22 is available, the department shall:
23 (1) provide reasonable notice to all lenders identifying the
24 commercially reasonable method of verification that is
25 available; and
26 (2) require each lender to use, consistent with the policies of
27 the department, the identified commercially reasonable
28 method of verification as a means of complying with
29 subsection (d).
30 (f) If a borrower presents evidence to a lender that a loan has
31 been discharged in bankruptcy, the lender shall cause the record
32 of the borrower's loan to be updated in the data base described in
33 subsection (d)(2) to reflect the bankruptcy discharge.
34 (g) A lender shall cause the record of a borrower's loan to be
35 updated in the data base described in subsection (d)(2) to reflect:
36 (1) presentment of the borrower's check for payment; or
37 (2) exercise of the borrower's authorization to debit the
38 borrower's account.
39 If a check is returned or an authorization is dishonored because of
40 insufficient funds in the borrower's account, the lender shall
41 reenter the record of the loan in the data base.
42 (h) A lender shall update information in a data base described
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92
274
1 in subsection (d)(2) to reflect partial payments made on an
2 outstanding loan, the record of which is maintained in the data
3 base.
4 (i) If a lender ceases doing business in Indiana, the director may
5 require the operator of the data base described in subsection (d)(2)
6 to remove records of the lender's loans from the operator's data
7 base.
8 (j) The director may impose a civil penalty not to exceed one
9 hundred dollars ($100) for each violation of:
10 (1) this section; or
11 (2) any rule or policy adopted by the director to implement
12 this section.
13 (k) The excess amount of loan finance charge provided for in
14 agreements in violation of this section is an excess charge for
15 purposes of the provisions concerning effect of violations on rights
16 of parties (IC 37-2-6-9) and the provisions concerning civil actions
17 by the department (IC 37-2-7-14).
18 (l) If a borrower provides the borrower's Social Security
19 number to a lender in connection with any transaction or proposed
20 transaction under this chapter, the lender shall:
21 (1) maintain procedures to verify that the Social Security
22 number provided is legitimate and belongs to the borrower;
23 and
24 (2) retain copies of any documents used to verify the
25 borrower's Social Security number. Documentation under
26 this subdivision may be in electronic form and the numbers
27 may be truncated.
28 If a borrower does not have a Social Security number, the lender
29 may require and accept another valid form of government issued
30 identification, subject to the requirements of subdivisions (1) and
31 (2) with respect to the government issued identification accepted.
32 Sec. 9. (a) This section does not apply to a business that is
33 licensed by the department for a purpose other than consumer
34 loans.
35 (b) A licensee may carry on other business at a location where
36 the licensee makes small loans unless the licensee carries on other
37 business for the purpose of evasion or violation of this article.
38 Sec. 10. (a) An agreement with respect to a small loan may not
39 provide for charges as a result of default by the borrower other
40 than those specifically authorized by this chapter. A provision in a
41 small loan agreement in violation of this section is unenforceable.
42 (b) A lender or an assignee of a small loan may seek only the
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92
275
1 following remedies upon default by a borrower:
2 (1) Recovery of:
3 (A) the contracted principal amount of the loan; and
4 (B) the loan finance charge.
5 (2) If contracted for under section 3 of this chapter, collection
6 of a fee for:
7 (A) a returned check, negotiable order of withdrawal, or
8 share draft; or
9 (B) a dishonored authorization to debit the borrower's
10 account;
11 because of insufficient funds in the borrower's account.
12 (3) Collection of postjudgment interest, if awarded by a court.
13 (4) Collection of court costs, if awarded by a court.
14 (c) A lender or an assignee of a small loan may not seek any of
15 the following damages or remedies upon default by a borrower:
16 (1) Payment of the lender's attorney's fees.
17 (2) Treble damages.
18 (3) Prejudgment interest.
19 (4) Damages allowed for dishonored checks under any statute
20 other than this chapter.
21 (5) Any damages or remedies not set forth in subsection (b).
22 (d) A contractual agreement in a small loan transaction must
23 include a notice of the following in 14 point bold type:
24 (1) The remedies available to a lender or an assignee under
25 subsection (b).
26 (2) The remedies and damages that a lender or an assignee is
27 prohibited from seeking in a small loan transaction under
28 subsection (c).
29 Sec. 11. (a) This section applies to licensees and unlicensed
30 persons.
31 (b) A person who violates this chapter:
32 (1) is subject to the remedies provided in IC 37-2-6-9;
33 (2) commits a deceptive act under IC 24-5-0.5 and is subject
34 to the penalties listed in IC 24-5-0.5;
35 (3) has no right to collect, receive, or retain any principal,
36 interest, or other charges from a small loan; however, this
37 subdivision does not apply if the violation is the result of an
38 accident or bona fide error of computation; and
39 (4) is liable to the borrower for actual damages, statutory
40 damages of two thousand dollars ($2,000) per violation, costs,
41 and attorney's fees; however, this subdivision does not apply
42 if the violation is the result of an accident or bona fide error
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92
276
1 of computation.
2 The remedies described in this subsection are in addition to all
3 other remedies set forth in IC 37-2 and this article.
4 (c) The department may sue:
5 (1) to enjoin any conduct that constitutes or will constitute a
6 violation of this chapter; and
7 (2) for other equitable relief.
8 (d) The remedies provided in this section are cumulative but are
9 not intended to be the exclusive remedies available to a borrower.
10 A borrower is not required to exhaust any administrative remedies
11 under this section or any other applicable law.
12 Sec. 12. A lender making small loans, or an assignee of a small
13 loan, shall not commit nor cause to be committed any of the
14 following acts:
15 (1) Threatening to use or using the criminal process in any
16 state to collect on a small loan.
17 (2) Threatening to take action against a borrower that is
18 prohibited by this chapter.
19 (3) Making a misleading or deceptive statement regarding a
20 small loan or a consequence of taking a small loan.
21 (4) Contracting for or collecting attorney's fees on small loans
22 made under this chapter.
23 (5) Altering the date or any other information on a check or
24 an authorization to debit the borrower's account held as
25 security.
26 (6) Using a device or agreement that the department
27 determines would have the effect of charging or collecting
28 more fees, charges, or interest than allowed by this chapter,
29 including, but not limited to:
30 (A) entering a different type of transaction with the
31 borrower;
32 (B) entering into a sales/leaseback arrangement;
33 (C) catalog sales;
34 (D) entering into transactions in which a customer receives
35 a purported cash rebate that is advanced by someone
36 offering Internet content services, or some other product
37 or service, when the cash rebate does not represent a
38 discount or an adjustment of the purchase price for the
39 product or service; or
40 (E) entering any other transaction with the borrower that
41 is designed to evade the applicability of this chapter.
42 (7) Engaging in unfair, deceptive, or fraudulent practices in
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92
277
1 the making or collecting of a small loan.
2 (8) Charging to cash a check representing the proceeds of a
3 small loan.
4 (9) Except as otherwise provided in this chapter:
5 (A) accepting the proceeds of a new small loan as payment
6 of an existing small loan provided by the same lender; or
7 (B) renewing, refinancing, or consolidating a small loan
8 with the proceeds of another small loan made by the same
9 lender.
10 (10) Including any of the following provisions in a loan
11 document:
12 (A) A hold harmless clause.
13 (B) A confession of judgment clause.
14 (C) A mandatory arbitration clause, unless the terms and
15 conditions of the arbitration have been approved by the
16 director of the department.
17 (D) An assignment of or order for payment of wages or
18 other compensation for services.
19 (E) A provision in which the borrower agrees not to assert
20 a claim or defense arising out of contract.
21 (F) A waiver of any provision of this chapter.
22 (11) Selling insurance of any kind in connection with the
23 making or collecting of a small loan.
24 (12) Entering into a renewal with a borrower.
25 Sec. 13. Finance charges made in compliance with this chapter
26 are exempt from IC 37-2-4-35 and IC 35-45-7.
27 Sec. 14. Upon the receipt of a check from a borrower for a small
28 loan, unless the check is marked as void at the time of acceptance
29 by the lender, the lender shall immediately stamp the back of the
30 check with an endorsement that states:
31 "This check is being negotiated as part of a small loan under
32 IC 37-3, and any holder of this check takes it subject to the
33 claims and defenses of the maker.".
34 Sec. 15. (a) A person engaged in making small loans under this
35 chapter shall post a bond to the department in the amount of fifty
36 thousand dollars ($50,000) for each location where small loans will
37 be made, up to a maximum bond in an amount determined by the
38 director.
39 (b) A surety bond issued under this section must:
40 (1) provide coverage for a lender engaged in making small
41 loans under this chapter in an amount as prescribed in
42 subsection (a);
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92
278
1 (2) be in a form prescribed by the director;
2 (3) be in effect during the term of the lender's license under
3 this chapter;
4 (4) subject to subsection (c), remain in effect during the two
5 (2) years after the lender's license under this chapter is
6 surrendered or terminated;
7 (5) be payable to the department for the benefit of:
8 (A) the state; and
9 (B) individuals who reside in Indiana when they agree to
10 receive financial services from the lender;
11 (6) be issued by a bonding, surety, or insurance company
12 authorized to do business in Indiana and rated at least "A-"
13 by at least one (1) nationally recognized investment rating
14 service; and
15 (7) have payment conditioned upon the lender's or any of the
16 lender's employees' or agents' noncompliance with or
17 violation of this article or other applicable federal or state
18 laws or regulations.
19 (c) The director may adopt rules or guidance documents with
20 respect to the requirements for a surety bond as necessary to
21 accomplish the purposes of this chapter. Upon written request
22 from a lender, the director may, at the discretion of the director,
23 waive or shorten the two (2) year period set forth in subsection
24 (b)(4) during which a surety bond required by this section must
25 remain in effect after the lender's license under this chapter is
26 surrendered or terminated.
27 (d) If the principal amount of a surety bond required under this
28 section is reduced by payment of a claim or judgment, the lender
29 for whom the bond is issued shall immediately notify the director
30 of the reduction and, not later than thirty (30) days after notice by
31 the director, file a new or an additional surety bond in an amount
32 set by the director. The amount of the new or additional bond set
33 by the director must be at least the amount of the bond before
34 payment of the claim or judgment.
35 (e) If for any reason a surety terminates a bond issued under
36 this section, the lender shall immediately notify the department and
37 file a new surety bond in an amount as prescribed in subsection (a).
38 (f) Cancellation of a surety bond issued under this section does
39 not affect any liability incurred or accrued during the period when
40 the surety bond was in effect.
41 (g) The director may obtain satisfaction from a surety bond
42 issued under this section if the director incurs expenses, issues a
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92
279
1 final order, or recovers a final judgment under this chapter.
2 (h) Notices required under this section must be in writing and
3 delivered by certified mail, return receipt requested and postage
4 prepaid, or by overnight delivery using a nationally recognized
5 carrier.
6 Sec. 16. The department may adopt rules under IC 4-22-2 to
7 implement this article.
8 ARTICLE 4. MORTGAGE RESCUE PROTECTION FRAUD
9 Chapter 1. Application
10 Sec. 1. This article does not apply to the following:
11 (1) A person organized or chartered under the laws of this
12 state, any other state, or the United States that relate to a
13 bank, a trust company, a savings association, a savings bank,
14 a credit union, or an industrial loan and investment company.
15 (2) The Federal National Mortgage Association, the Federal
16 Home Loan Mortgage Corporation, or a Federal Home Loan
17 Bank.
18 (3) A department or agency of the United States or of Indiana.
19 (4) A person that is servicing or enforcing a loan that it owns.
20 (5) A person that is servicing a loan:
21 (A) for a person described in subdivisions (1) through (4);
22 or
23 (B) insured by the Department of Housing and Urban
24 Development or guaranteed by the Veterans
25 Administration.
26 (6) An attorney licensed to practice law in Indiana who is
27 representing a mortgagor.
28 Chapter 2. Definitions
29 Sec. 1. The definitions in this chapter apply throughout this
30 article.
31 Sec. 2. "Foreclosure consultant" means a person who, directly
32 or indirectly, makes a solicitation, a representation, or an offer to
33 a homeowner to perform, with or without compensation, any
34 service that the person represents will:
35 (1) prevent or postpone the initiation of a foreclosure
36 proceeding, or reverse the effect of a foreclosure proceeding;
37 (2) allow the homeowner to become a lessee or renter in the
38 homeowner's residence during or after a foreclosure
39 proceeding; or
40 (3) allow the homeowner to have an option to repurchase the
41 homeowner's residence after a foreclosure proceeding.
42 Sec. 3. "Foreclosure purchaser" means a person who purchases
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92
280
1 real property in a foreclosure proceeding.
2 Sec. 4. "Foreclosure reconveyance" means a transaction
3 involving:
4 (1) the transfer of interest in real property by a homeowner
5 to a person during or incident to a proposed foreclosure
6 proceeding, either by:
7 (A) transfer of interest from the homeowner to the person;
8 or
9 (B) creation of a mortgage, trust, or other lien or
10 encumbrance during the foreclosure process;
11 that allows the person to obtain legal or equitable title to all
12 or part of the real property; and
13 (2) the subsequent conveyance, or promise of subsequent
14 conveyance, of interest back to the homeowner by the person
15 or the person's agent that allows the homeowner to possess
16 the real property following the completion of the foreclosure
17 proceeding.
18 Sec. 5. "Formal settlement" means a face-to-face meeting with
19 a homeowner to complete final documents incident to the:
20 (1) sale or transfer of real property; or
21 (2) creation of a mortgage or equitable interest in real
22 property;
23 conducted by a person who is not employed by or an affiliate of the
24 foreclosure purchaser.
25 Sec. 6. "Homeowner" means a person who holds record title to
26 residential real property as of the date on which:
27 (1) a contract with a foreclosure consultant; or
28 (2) a foreclosure reconveyance agreement;
29 with respect to the residential real property is entered into.
30 Chapter 3. Rescission of Contracts With Foreclosure
31 Consultants and Foreclosure Reconveyance Agreements
32 Sec. 1. In addition to any other right under law to cancel or
33 rescind a contract, a homeowner may rescind a:
34 (1) contract with a foreclosure consultant at any time before
35 midnight of the seventh business day after the date the
36 contract is signed; and
37 (2) foreclosure reconveyance agreement at any time before
38 midnight of the seventh business day after the homeowner's
39 transfer of the interest in the real property that is the subject
40 of the agreement, as described in IC 37-4-2-4(1).
41 Sec. 2. A homeowner effectively rescinds a contract with a
42 foreclosure consultant if the homeowner gives written notice of a
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92
281
1 rescission to the foreclosure consultant by one (1) of the following:
2 (1) Mailing the rescission to the address specified in the
3 contract.
4 (2) Sending the rescission through any facsimile or electronic
5 mail address identified in the contract or other material
6 provided to the homeowner by the foreclosure consultant.
7 Sec. 3. (a) If a notice of rescission under this chapter is sent by
8 mail, the rescission is effective three (3) days after the notice is
9 deposited in the United States mail, properly addressed, with
10 postage prepaid.
11 (b) A homeowner is not required to give notice of rescission in
12 the form required under the contract if the form under the
13 contract is inconsistent with the requirements under this chapter.
14 Sec. 4. (a) If a homeowner rescinds a contract with a foreclosure
15 consultant or a foreclosure reconveyance agreement, the
16 homeowner shall, not later than thirty (30) days after the date of
17 rescission, repay any amounts paid or advanced by:
18 (1) the foreclosure consultant or the foreclosure consultant's
19 agent under the terms of the foreclosure consulting contract;
20 or
21 (2) a person under a foreclosure reconveyance agreement.
22 (b) A rescission by a homeowner under this chapter is void if the
23 payments required under this section are not made within the time
24 set forth in subsection (a).
25 Sec. 5. If a homeowner rescinds a contract with a foreclosure
26 consultant, not less than ten (10) days following the effective date
27 of the rescission, the consultant shall return to the homeowner any
28 payments made by the homeowner, less any amounts for actual
29 services rendered.
30 Chapter 4. Limitations on Foreclosure Consultants and
31 Foreclosure Reconveyances
32 Sec. 1. For purposes of this chapter, there is a rebuttable
33 presumption that:
34 (1) a homeowner has a reasonable ability to pay for a
35 subsequent reconveyance of real property if the homeowner's
36 payments for primary housing expenses and regular principal
37 and interest payments on other personal debt, on a monthly
38 basis, do not exceed sixty percent (60%) of the homeowner's
39 monthly gross income; and
40 (2) the foreclosure purchaser has not verified reasonable
41 payment ability if the foreclosure purchaser has not obtained
42 documents other than a statement by the homeowner of
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92
282
1 assets, liability, and income.
2 Sec. 2. In addition to any prohibitions that apply under
3 IC 24-5-15-1 through IC 24-5-15-8, a foreclosure consultant may
4 not:
5 (1) enter into or attempt to enter into a foreclosure consultant
6 contract with a homeowner unless the foreclosure consultant
7 first provides the homeowner written notice of the
8 homeowner's rights under this article;
9 (2) demand or receive compensation until after the
10 foreclosure consultant has fully performed all services the
11 foreclosure consultant contracted to perform or represented
12 that the foreclosure consultant would perform, unless the
13 foreclosure consultant complies with the security
14 requirements under IC 24-5-15-8;
15 (3) demand or receive a fee, interest, or any other
16 compensation that exceeds eight percent (8%) per year of the
17 amount of any loan that the foreclosure consultant makes to
18 the homeowner;
19 (4) take a wage assignment, a lien of any type on real or
20 personal property, or any other security to secure the
21 payment of compensation;
22 (5) receive consideration from a third party in connection
23 with foreclosure consulting services provided to a homeowner
24 unless the consideration is first fully disclosed in writing to the
25 homeowner;
26 (6) acquire any interest, directly or indirectly, in residential
27 real property in foreclosure from a homeowner with whom
28 the foreclosure consultant has contracted;
29 (7) except to inspect documents as provided by law, take any
30 power of attorney from a homeowner for any purpose;
31 (8) execute any contract or agreement with a homeowner or
32 receive money or other valuable consideration from a
33 homeowner without providing the homeowner with the
34 written statement required by IC 24-5-15-6; or
35 (9) fail to provide a homeowner with a written contract that
36 includes the notice of cancellation required by IC 24-5-15-7.
37 Sec. 3. A foreclosure purchaser may not enter into or attempt to
38 enter into a foreclosure reconveyance agreement with a
39 homeowner unless the:
40 (1) foreclosure purchaser verifies and demonstrates that the
41 homeowner has or will have a reasonable ability to:
42 (A) pay for the subsequent reconveyance of the property
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92
283
1 back to the homeowner on completion of the terms of the
2 foreclosure conveyance; or
3 (B) if the foreclosure conveyance provides for a lease with
4 an option to repurchase the real property, make the lease
5 payment and repurchase the real property within the
6 period of the option to repurchase;
7 (2) foreclosure purchaser provides the homeowner written
8 notice of the homeowner's rights under this article;
9 (3) foreclosure purchaser and the homeowner complete a
10 formal settlement before any transfer of interest in the
11 affected property; and
12 (4) foreclosure purchaser complies with the security
13 requirements under IC 24-5-15-8.
14 Sec. 4. A foreclosure purchaser shall:
15 (1) ensure that title to real property has been reconveyed to
16 the homeowner in a timely manner if the terms of a
17 foreclosure reconveyance agreement require a reconveyance;
18 or
19 (2) if the real property subject to a foreclosure reconveyance
20 agreement is sold within eighteen (18) months after entering
21 into the foreclosure reconveyance agreement, make payment
22 to the homeowner not later than ninety (90) days after the
23 resale of the real property in an amount equal to at least
24 sixty-six percent (66%) of the net proceeds from the resale of
25 the property.
26 Sec. 5. A foreclosure purchaser may not:
27 (1) enter into repurchase or lease terms as part of the
28 foreclosure reconveyance that are unfair or commercially
29 unreasonable or engage in any other unfair conduct;
30 (2) represent, directly or indirectly, that the:
31 (A) foreclosure purchaser is acting:
32 (i) as an adviser or a consultant; or
33 (ii) in any other manner on behalf of the homeowner;
34 (B) foreclosure purchaser is assisting the homeowner to
35 save the residence; or
36 (C) foreclosure purchaser is assisting the homeowner in
37 preventing a foreclosure if the result of the transaction is
38 that the homeowner will not complete a redemption of the
39 property; or
40 (3) until the homeowner's right to rescind or cancel the
41 foreclosure reconveyance agreement has expired:
42 (A) record any document, including an instrument or
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92
284
1 conveyance, signed by the homeowner; or
2 (B) transfer to a third party or encumber, or purport to
3 transfer to a third party or encumber, any interest in the
4 residential real property in foreclosure.
5 Sec. 6. A foreclosure purchaser shall make a detailed accounting
6 of the basis for the amount of payment made to a homeowner of
7 real property resold within eighteen (18) months after entering into
8 a foreclosure reconveyance agreement on a form prescribed by the
9 attorney general.
10 Sec. 7. A foreclosure consultant shall retain all records and
11 documents, including the foreclosure consultant contract, related
12 to services performed on behalf of a homeowner for at least three
13 (3) years after the termination or conclusion of the foreclosure
14 consultant contract entered into by the foreclosure consultant and
15 the homeowner.
16 Sec. 8. A foreclosure consultant may not represent to a
17 homeowner that the foreclosure consultant is endorsed, sponsored,
18 or affiliated with any governmental or government sponsored
19 agency or program.
20 Chapter 5. Enforcement
21 Sec. 1. A person who knowingly or intentionally violates this
22 article commits:
23 (1) a Class A misdemeanor;
24 (2) a deceptive act that is actionable by the attorney general
25 under IC 24-5-0.5-4 and is subject to the penalties and
26 remedies available to the attorney general under IC 24-5-0.5;
27 and
28 (3) a deceptive act that is actionable by the attorney general
29 under IC 37-5-8-2 and is subject to the penalties and remedies
30 available to the attorney general under IC 37-5.
31 Sec. 2. (a) A homeowner may bring an action against a person
32 for damages incurred as a result of a violation of this article.
33 (b) A homeowner who:
34 (1) brings an action under this section; and
35 (2) is awarded damages;
36 may seek reasonable attorney's fees.
37 Sec. 3. (a) A court may award attorney's fees under section 2(b)
38 of this chapter.
39 (b) If the court finds that a person willfully or knowingly
40 violated this article, the court may award damages equal to three
41 (3) times the amount of actual damages.
42 Sec. 4. (a) The Indiana housing and community development
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92
285
1 authority shall maintain a list of nonprofit organizations that:
2 (1) offer counseling or advice to homeowners in foreclosure or
3 loan defaults; and
4 (2) do not contract for services with for-profit lenders or
5 foreclosure purchasers.
6 (b) The Indiana housing and community development authority
7 shall provide names and telephone numbers of the organizations
8 described in subsection (a) to a homeowner upon request.
9 Sec. 5. The attorney general may adopt rules under IC 4-22-2
10 necessary to implement this article.
11 Sec. 6. Except as provided in IC 24-5-15-7(d), this article may
12 not be construed to preempt the provisions of IC 24-5-15-1 through
13 IC 24-5-15-11.
14 ARTICLE 5. HOME LOAN PRACTICES
15 Chapter 1. Application
16 Sec. 1. Except for IC 37-5-3-7(c)(3), IC 37-5-3-7(c)(4), and
17 IC 37-5-3-7(c)(5), this article does not apply to:
18 (1) a loan made or acquired by a person organized or
19 chartered under the laws of this state, any other state, or the
20 United States relating to banks, trust companies, savings
21 associations, savings banks, credit unions, or industrial loan
22 and investment companies; or
23 (2) a loan:
24 (A) that can be purchased by the Federal National
25 Mortgage Association, the Federal Home Loan Mortgage
26 Association, or the Federal Home Loan Bank;
27 (B) to be insured by the United States Department of
28 Housing and Urban Development;
29 (C) to be guaranteed by the United States Department of
30 Veterans Affairs;
31 (D) to be made or guaranteed by the United States
32 Department of Agriculture Rural Housing Service;
33 (E) to be funded by the Indiana housing and community
34 development authority; or
35 (F) with a principal amount that exceeds the conforming
36 loan size limit for a single family dwelling as established by
37 the Federal National Mortgage Association.
38 Chapter 2. Definitions
39 Sec. 1. The definitions in this chapter apply throughout this
40 article.
41 Sec. 2. "Benchmark rate" means the interest rate established
42 under Section 152 of the Federal Home Ownership and Equity
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92
286
1 Protection Act of 1994 (15 U.S.C. 1602 (aa)) and the regulations
2 adopted under that act by the Federal Reserve Board, including 12
3 CFR 226.32 and the Official Staff Commentary to the regulations
4 as amended.
5 Sec. 3. "Bona fide discount points" means loan discount points
6 that:
7 (1) are knowingly paid by the borrower;
8 (2) are paid for the express purpose of reducing the interest
9 rate applicable to the loan;
10 (3) reduce the interest rate from an interest rate that does not
11 exceed the benchmark rate; and
12 (4) are recouped within the first four (4) years of the
13 scheduled loan payments;
14 if the reduction in the interest rate that is achieved by the payment
15 of the loan discount points reduces the interest charged on the
16 scheduled payments so that the borrower's dollar amount of
17 savings in interest during the first four (4) years of the loan is equal
18 to or greater than the dollar amount of loan discount points paid
19 by the borrower.
20 Sec. 4. "Borrower" means a person obligated to repay a home
21 loan, including a coborrower, cosigner, or guarantor.
22 Sec. 5. "Bridge loan" means temporary or short term financing
23 with a maturity of less than eighteen (18) months that requires
24 payments of interest only until the entire unpaid balance is due and
25 payable.
26 Sec. 6. (a) "Creditor" means:
27 (1) a person:
28 (A) who regularly extends consumer credit that is subject
29 to a finance charge or that is payable by written agreement
30 in more than four (4) installments; and
31 (B) to whom the debt arising from a home loan transaction
32 is initially payable; or
33 (2) a person who brokers a home loan, including a person
34 who:
35 (A) directly or indirectly solicits, processes, places, or
36 negotiates home loans for others;
37 (B) offers to solicit, process, place, or negotiate home loans
38 for others; or
39 (C) closes home loans that may be in the person's own
40 name with funds provided by others and that are
41 thereafter assigned to the person providing funding for the
42 loans.
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92
287
1 (b) The term does not include:
2 (1) a servicer;
3 (2) a state or local housing finance authority;
4 (3) any other state or local governmental or
5 quasi-governmental entity; or
6 (4) an attorney providing legal services in association with the
7 closing of a home loan.
8 Sec. 7. (a) "Deceptive act" means:
9 (1) an act or a practice as part of a mortgage transaction (as
10 defined in IC 37-5-3-7(a)), or of a real estate transaction (as
11 defined in IC 37-5-3-7(b)), in which a person at the time of the
12 transaction knowingly or intentionally:
13 (A) makes a material misrepresentation; or
14 (B) conceals material information regarding the terms or
15 conditions of the transaction; or
16 (2) a violation of IC 37-4 concerning mortgage rescue
17 protection fraud as set forth in IC 37-4-5-1.
