16 Sec. 35. (a) With respect to a supervised loan, including a loan
17 pursuant to a revolving loan account, a supervised lender may
18 contract for and receive a loan finance charge not exceeding that
19 permitted by this section.
20 (b) The loan finance charge, calculated according to the
21 actuarial method, may not exceed the equivalent of the greater of:
22 (1) the total of:
23 (A) thirty-six percent (36%) per year on that part of the
24 unpaid balances of the principal which is two thousand
25 dollars ($2,000) or less;
26 (B) twenty-one percent (21%) per year on that part of the
27 unpaid balances of the principal which is more than two
28 thousand dollars ($2,000) but does not exceed four
29 thousand dollars ($4,000); and
30 (C) fifteen percent (15%) per year on that part of the
31 unpaid balances of the principal which is more than four
32 thousand dollars ($4,000); or
33 (2) twenty-five percent (25%) per year on the unpaid balances
34 of the principal.
35 (c) In the case of a loan agreement entered into before July 1,
36 2020, this section does not limit or restrict the manner of
37 contracting for the loan finance charge, whether by way of add-on,
38 discount, or otherwise, so long as the rate of the loan finance
39 charge does not exceed that permitted by this section. If the loan is
40 precomputed:
41 (1) the loan finance charge may be calculated on the
42 assumption that all scheduled payments will be made when
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

225
1 due; and
2 (2) the effect of prepayment is governed by the provisions on
3 rebate upon prepayment in section 13 of this chapter.
4 After June 30, 2020, a loan agreement may not be entered into for
5 a precomputed supervised loan. The loan finance charge
6 authorized by this section must be contracted for between the
7 lender and the debtor, and must be calculated by applying a rate
8 not exceeding the rate set forth in subsection (b) to unpaid balances
9 of the principal.
10 (d) The term of a loan for the purposes of this section
11 commences on the date the loan is made. Differences in the lengths
12 of months are disregarded, and a day may be counted as
13 one-thirtieth (1/30) of a month. Subject to classifications and
14 differentiations the lender may reasonably establish, a part of a
15 month in excess of fifteen (15) days may be treated as a full month
16 if periods of fifteen (15) days or less are disregarded and that
17 procedure is not consistently used to obtain a greater yield than
18 would otherwise be permitted.
19 (e) Subject to classifications and differentiations the lender may
20 reasonably establish, the lender may make the same loan finance
21 charge on all principal amounts within a specified range. A loan
22 finance charge does not violate subsection (b) if:
23 (1) when applied to the median amount within each range, it
24 does not exceed the maximum permitted in subsection (b);
25 and
26 (2) when applied to the lowest amount within each range, it
27 does not produce a rate of loan finance charge exceeding the
28 rate calculated according to subdivision (1) by more than
29 eight percent (8%) of the rate calculated according to
30 subdivision (1).
31 (f) The amounts of two thousand dollars ($2,000) and four
32 thousand dollars ($4,000) in subsection (b) and thirty dollars ($30)
33 in subsection (g) are subject to change pursuant to the provisions
34 on adjustment of dollar amounts set forth in IC 37-2-1-5. However,
35 notwithstanding IC 37-2-1-5(a), for the adjustment of the amount
36 of thirty dollars ($30), the Reference Base Index to be used is the
37 Index for October 1992. Notwithstanding IC 37-2-1-5(a), for the
38 adjustment of the amounts of two thousand dollars ($2,000) and
39 four thousand dollars ($4,000), the Reference Base Index to be used
40 is the Index for October 2012.
41 (g) With respect to a supervised loan not made pursuant to a
42 revolving loan account, the lender may contract for and receive a
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

226
1 minimum loan finance charge of not more than thirty dollars ($30).
2 The minimum loan finance charge allowed under this subsection
3 may be imposed only if the lender does not assess a nonrefundable
4 prepaid finance charge under subsection (h) and:
5 (1) the debtor prepays in full a consumer loan, refinancing, or
6 consolidation, regardless of whether the loan, refinancing, or
7 consolidation is precomputed;
8 (2) the loan, refinancing, or consolidation prepaid by the
9 debtor is subject to a loan finance charge that:
10 (A) is contracted for by the parties; and
11 (B) does not exceed the rate prescribed in subsection (b);
12 and
13 (3) the loan finance charge earned at the time of prepayment
14 is less than the minimum loan finance charge contracted for
15 under this subsection.
16 (h) Except as provided in subsections (g) and (j)(3), in addition
17 to the loan finance charge provided for in this section and to any
18 other charges and fees permitted by this chapter, the lender may
19 contract for and receive a nonrefundable prepaid finance charge
20 of not more than fifty dollars ($50) if the loan agreement is entered
21 into before July 1, 2020. If the loan agreement is entered into after
22 June 30, 2020, not more than the following:
23 (1) Seventy-five dollars ($75), in the case of a loan agreement
24 for a principal amount which is two thousand dollars ($2,000)
25 or less.
26 (2) One hundred fifty dollars ($150) in the case of a loan
27 agreement for a principal amount which is more than two
28 thousand dollars ($2,000) but does not exceed four thousand
29 dollars ($4,000).
30 (3) Two hundred dollars ($200) in the case of a loan
31 agreement for a principal amount which is more than four
32 thousand dollars ($4,000).
33 The amounts in this subsection are not subject to change under
34 IC 37-2-1-5.
35 (i) The nonrefundable prepaid finance charge provided for in
36 subsection (h) is not subject to refund or rebate. However, for any
37 supervised loan entered into after June 30, 2020, any amount
38 charged by the lender, other than by a lender that is a depository
39 institution, under subsection (h) that exceeds the applicable amount
40 permitted by subsection (h) constitutes a violation of this article
41 under IC 37-2-7-8(12) and is subject to refund. Any amount
42 charged by a depository institution under subsection (h) that
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

227
1 exceeds the applicable amount set forth in subsection (h) is subject
2 to refund.
3 (j) Notwithstanding subsections (h) and (i), in the case of a
4 supervised loan that is not secured by an interest in land, if a
5 lender retains any part of a nonrefundable prepaid finance charge
6 charged on a loan that is paid in full by a new loan from the same
7 lender, the following apply:
8 (1) If the loan is paid in full by the new loan within three (3)
9 months after the date of the prior loan, the lender may not
10 charge a nonrefundable prepaid finance charge on the new
11 loan, or, in the case of a revolving loan, on the increased credit
12 line.
13 (2) The lender may not assess more than two (2)
14 nonrefundable prepaid finance charges in any twelve (12)
15 month period.
16 (3) Subject to subdivisions (a) and (b), if a supervised loan
17 that is entered into by a lender and a debtor before July 1,
18 2020, is paid in full by a new loan from the same lender after
19 June 30, 2020, the lender may contract for and receive a
20 nonrefundable prepaid finance charge in the amount set forth
21 in subsection (h) for loan agreements entered into after June
22 30, 2020.
23 (k) In the case of a supervised loan that is secured by an interest
24 in land, this section does not prohibit a lender from contracting for
25 and receiving a fee for preparing deeds, mortgages, reconveyances,
26 and similar documents under section 5(a)(4)(B) of this chapter, in
27 addition to the nonrefundable prepaid finance charge provided for
28 in subsection (h).
29 Sec. 36. With respect to a consumer loan, no lender may permit
30 any person, or husband and wife, to become obligated in any way
31 under more than one loan agreement with the lender or with a
32 person related to the lender, with intent to obtain a higher rate of
33 loan finance charge than would otherwise be permitted by the
34 provisions on loan finance charge for supervised loans set forth in
35 section 35 of this chapter or to avoid disclosure of an annual
36 percentage rate pursuant to the provisions on disclosure set forth
37 in section 14 of this chapter. The excess amount of loan finance
38 charge provided for in agreements in violation of this section is an
39 excess charge for the purposes of the provisions on effect of
40 violations on rights of parties (IC 37-2-6-9) and the provisions on
41 civil actions by the department (IC 37-2-7-14).
42 Sec. 37. (a) With respect to a supervised loan in which the
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

228
1 principal is four thousand dollars ($4,000) or less, a lender may not
2 contract for an interest in land as security. A security interest
3 taken in violation of this section is void.
4 (b) The amount of four thousand dollars ($4,000) in subsection
5 (a) is subject to change pursuant to the provisions on adjustment
6 of dollar amounts set forth in IC 37-2-1-5. However,
7 notwithstanding IC 37-2-1-5(a), the Reference Base Index to be
8 used under this subsection is the Index for October 2012.
9 Sec. 38. (a) Supervised loans not made pursuant to a revolving
10 loan account and in which the principal is four thousand dollars
11 ($4,000) or less are payable in a single installment or shall be
12 scheduled to be payable in substantially equal installments that are
13 payable at equal periodic intervals, except to the extent that the
14 schedule of payments is adjusted to the seasonal or irregular
15 income of the debtor, and:
16 (1) over a period of not more than thirty-seven (37) months if
17 the principal is more than three hundred dollars ($300); or
18 (2) over a period of not more than twenty-five (25) months if
19 the principal is three hundred dollars ($300) or less.
20 (b) The amounts of three hundred dollars ($300) and four
21 thousand dollars ($4,000) in subsection (a) are subject to change
22 pursuant to the provisions on adjustment of dollar amounts set
23 forth in IC 37-2-1-5. However, notwithstanding IC 37-2-1-5(a), the
24 Reference Base Index to be used with respect to the amount of:
25 (1) three hundred dollars ($300) is the Index for October
26 1992; and
27 (2) four thousand dollars ($4,000) is the Index for October
28 2012.
29 Sec. 39. A licensee may carry on other business at a location
30 where the licensee makes consumer loans unless the licensee
31 carries on other business for the purpose of evasion or violation of
32 this article.
33 Sec. 40. (a) As used in this section, "control" means possession
34 of the power directly or indirectly to:
35 (1) direct or cause the direction of the management or policies
36 of a creditor, whether through the beneficial ownership of
37 voting securities, by contract, or otherwise; or
38 (2) vote at least twenty-five percent (25%) of the voting
39 securities of a creditor, whether the voting rights are derived
40 through the beneficial ownership of voting securities, by
41 contract, or otherwise.
42 (b) An organization or an individual acting directly, indirectly,
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

229
1 or through or in concert with one (1) or more other organizations
2 or individuals may not acquire control of any creditor unless the
3 department has received and approved an application for change
4 in control. The department has not more than one hundred twenty
5 (120) days after receipt of an application to issue a notice
6 approving the proposed change in control. The application must
7 contain the name and address of the organization, individual, or
8 individuals who propose to acquire control and any other
9 information required by the director.
10 (c) The period for approval under subsection (b) may be
11 extended:
12 (1) in the discretion of the director for an additional thirty
13 (30) days; and
14 (2) not more than two (2) additional times for not more than
15 forty-five (45) days each time if:
16 (A) the director determines that the organization,
17 individual, or individuals who propose to acquire control
18 have not submitted substantial evidence of the
19 qualifications described in subsection (d);
20 (B) the director determines that any material information
21 submitted is substantially inaccurate; or
22 (C) the director has been unable to complete the
23 investigation of the organization, individual, or individuals
24 who propose to acquire control because of any delay
25 caused by or the inadequate cooperation of the
26 organization, individual, or individuals.
27 (d) The department shall issue a notice approving the
28 application only after the department is satisfied that both of the
29 following apply:
30 (1) The organization, individual, or individuals who propose
31 to acquire control are qualified by competence, experience,
32 character, and financial responsibility to control and operate
33 the creditor in a legal and proper manner.
34 (2) The interests of the owners and creditors of the creditor
35 and the interests of the public generally will not be
36 jeopardized by the proposed change in control.
37 (e) The director may determine, in the director's discretion, that
38 subsection (b) does not apply to a transaction if the director
39 determines that the direct or beneficial ownership of the creditor
40 will not change as a result of the transaction.
41 (f) The president or other chief executive officer of a creditor
42 shall report to the director any transfer or sale of securities of the
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

230
1 creditor that results in direct or indirect ownership by a holder or
2 an affiliated group of holders of at least ten percent (10%) of the
3 outstanding securities of the creditor. The report required by this
4 subsection must be made not later than ten (10) days after the
5 transfer of the securities on the books of the creditor.
6 (g) Depending on the circumstances of the transaction, the
7 director may reserve the right to require the organization,
8 individual, or individuals who propose to acquire control of a
9 creditor licensed under this article to apply for a new license under
10 section 26 of this chapter, instead of acquiring control of the
11 licensee under this section.
12 Sec. 41. The parties to a loan other than a consumer loan may
13 agree in writing signed by the parties that the loan is subject to the
14 provisions of this article applying to consumer loans. If the parties
15 so agree, the loan is a consumer loan for the purposes of this
16 article.
17 Sec. 42. (a) With respect to a consumer related loan, including
18 one made pursuant to a revolving loan account, the parties may
19 contract for the payment by the debtor of a loan finance charge,
20 calculated according to the actuarial method, not to exceed
21 twenty-five percent (25%) per year on the unpaid principal
22 balance.
23 (b) A person engaged in consumer related loans is not required
24 to comply with:
25 (1) the licensing requirements set forth in section 26 of this
26 chapter; or
27 (2) IC 37-2-7-24 through IC 37-2-7-26.
28 Sec. 43. (a) The agreement with respect to a consumer related
29 loan may provide for only the following charges as a result of the
30 debtor's default:
31 (1) reasonable attorney's fees and reasonable expenses
32 incurred in realizing on a security interest;
33 (2) deferral charges not in excess of twenty-five percent (25%)
34 per year of the amount deferred for the period of deferral;
35 and
36 (3) other charges that could have been made had the loan
37 been a consumer loan.
38 (b) A provision in violation of this section is unenforceable.
39 Sec. 44. With respect to a loan other than a consumer loan or a
40 consumer related loan, the parties may contract for the payment
41 by the debtor of any loan finance charge.
42 Sec. 45. (a) In addition to any disclosures otherwise provided by
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

231
1 law, a lender soliciting loans using a negotiable check, facsimile, or
2 other negotiable instrument that may be used by a consumer to
3 activate a new loan shall disclose the following:
4 "This is a solicitation for a loan. Read the enclosed disclosures
5 before signing this agreement.".
6 This notice shall be printed in at least ten point type and shall
7 appear conspicuously on the offer.
8 (b) If a negotiable check, a facsimile, or another instrument is
9 stolen or incorrectly received by someone other than the intended
10 payee and the instrument is fraudulently cashed, the consumer who
11 was the intended payee is not liable for the loan obligation.
12 Sec. 46. With respect to a consumer loan secured by an interest
13 in land used or expected to be used as the principal dwelling of the
14 debtor, a lender shall comply with IC 6-1.1-12-43.
15 Chapter 5. Insurance
16 Sec. 1. (a) Except as provided in subsection (b), this chapter
17 applies to insurance provided or to be provided in relation to a
18 consumer credit sale, a consumer lease, or a consumer loan.
19 (b) Section 17 of this chapter concerning cancellation by a
20 creditor applies to loans the primary purpose of which is the
21 financing of insurance. No other provision of this chapter applies
22 to insurance so financed.
23 (c) This chapter supplements and does not repeal IC 27-8-4 (the
24 credit insurance act). The provisions of this article concerning
25 administrative controls, liabilities, and penalties do not apply to
26 persons acting as insurers, and the similar provisions of IC 27-8-4
27 do not apply to creditors and debtors.
28 Sec. 2. (a) Except as otherwise provided in this chapter and
29 subject to the provisions on:
30 (1) additional charges set forth in IC 37-2-3-5 and
31 IC 37-2-4-5;
32 (2) maximum charges set forth in IC 37-2-3-4 through
33 IC 37-2-3-12; and
34 (3) maximum charges set forth in IC 37-2-4-4 through
35 IC 37-2-4-13;
36 a creditor may agree to provide insurance, and may contract for
37 and receive a charge for insurance separate from and in addition
38 to other charges. A creditor need not make a separate charge for
39 insurance provided or required by the creditor. This article does
40 not authorize the issuance of any insurance prohibited under any
41 statute, or rule thereunder, governing the business of insurance.
42 (b) The excess amount of a charge for insurance provided for in
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

232
1 agreements in violation of this chapter is an excess charge for the
2 purposes of the provisions of the chapter on remedies and penalties
3 (IC 37-2-6) as to effect of violations on rights of parties (IC
4 37-2-6-9) and of the provisions of IC 37-2-7 as to civil actions by
5 the department (IC 37-2-7-14).
6 Sec. 3. If a creditor agrees with a debtor to provide insurance:
7 (1) the insurance shall be evidenced by an individual policy or
8 certificate of insurance delivered to the debtor, or sent to the
9 debtor at the debtor's address as stated by the debtor, within
10 thirty (30) days after the term of the insurance commences
11 under the agreement between the creditor and debtor; or
12 (2) the creditor shall promptly notify the debtor of any failure
13 or delay in providing the insurance.
14 Sec. 4. (a) In applying the provisions of this article on
15 unconscionability (IC 37-2-6-7 and IC 37-2-7-12) to a separate
16 charge for insurance, consideration shall be given, among other
17 factors, to:
18 (1) potential benefits to the debtor including the satisfaction
19 of the debtor's obligations;
20 (2) the creditor's need for the protection provided by the
21 insurance; and
22 (3) the relation between the amount and terms of credit
23 granted and the insurance benefits provided.
24 (b) If consumer credit insurance otherwise complies with this
25 chapter and other applicable law, neither the amount nor the term
26 of the insurance nor the amount of a charge therefor is in itself
27 unconscionable.
28 Sec. 5. (a) Except as provided in subsection (b), if a creditor
29 contracts for or receives a separate charge for insurance, the
30 amount charged to the debtor for the insurance may not exceed the
31 premium to be charged by the insurer, as computed at the time the
32 charge to the debtor is determined, conforming to any rate filings
33 required by law and made by the insurer with the insurance
34 commissioner.
35 (b) A creditor who provides consumer credit insurance in
36 relation to a revolving charge account or revolving loan account
37 may calculate the charge to the debtor in each billing cycle by
38 applying the current premium rate to one (1) of the following:
39 (1) The average daily unpaid balance of the debt in the cycle.
40 (2) The unpaid balance of the debt or a median amount within
41 a specified range of unpaid balances of debt on approximately
42 the same day of the cycle. The day of the cycle need not be the
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

233
1 day used in calculating the credit service charge under
2 IC 37-2-3-4(f) or loan finance charge (IC 37-2-4-4 and
3 IC 37-2-4-35), but the specified range shall be the range used
4 for that purpose.
5 (3) The unpaid balances of principal calculated according to
6 the actuarial method.
7 (4) The amount of the insurance benefit for the cycle.
8 Sec. 6. (a) Upon prepayment in full of a consumer credit sale or
9 consumer loan by the proceeds of consumer credit insurance, the
10 debtor or the debtor's estate is entitled to a refund of:
11 (1) any portion of a separate charge for insurance which by
12 reason of prepayment is retained by the creditor or returned
13 to the creditor by the insurer unless the charge was computed
14 from time to time on the basis of the balances of the debtor's
15 account; and
16 (2) any portion of an additional charge that is:
17 (A) assessed in accordance with IC 37-2-3-5(a)(3),
18 IC 37-2-3-5(a)(8), IC 37-2-3-5(a)(5), or IC 37-2-4-5(a)(11);
19 and
20 (B) subject to rebate upon prepayment.
21 (b) This chapter does not require a creditor to grant a refund or
22 credit to the debtor if all refunds and credits due to the debtor
23 under this chapter amount to less than one dollar ($1), and except
24 as provided in subsection (a) does not require the creditor to
25 account to the debtor for any portion of a separate charge for
26 insurance because:
27 (1) the insurance is terminated by performance of the
28 insurer's obligation;
29 (2) the creditor pays or accounts for premiums to the insurer
30 in amounts and at times determined by the agreement
31 between them; or
32 (3) the creditor receives directly or indirectly under any
33 policy of insurance a gain or advantage not prohibited by law.
34 (c) Except as provided in subsection (b), the creditor or the
35 creditor's assignee shall promptly make an appropriate refund or
36 credit to the debtor for any separate charge made for insurance or
37 for an additional charge described in subsection (a)(2) if:
38 (1) the insurance is not provided or is provided for a term
39 shorter than the term for which the charge to the debtor for
40 insurance was computed; or
41 (2) the insurance or the protection provided in exchange for
42 the additional charge described in subsection (a)(2) terminates
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

234
1 prior to the end of the scheduled term of the coverage because
2 of prepayment in full or otherwise.
3 (d) An initial creditor, a subsequent creditor, or an assignee of
4 an initial or a subsequent creditor, shall maintain documentation
5 of any account that is subject to a refund or credit under this
6 section. The information maintained under this subsection shall be
7 made available to the department as necessary to determine
8 compliance with this section.
9 (e) A refund or credit required by subsection (c)(1) is
10 appropriate as to amount if it is computed according to a method
11 prescribed or approved by the insurance commissioner or a
12 formula filed by the insurer with the insurance commissioner at
13 least thirty (30) days before the debtor's right to a refund or credit
14 becomes determinable, unless the method or formula is used after
15 the insurance commissioner notifies the insurer that it is
16 disapproved.
17 (f) If a refund or credit required by subsection (a) or (c) is not
18 made to the debtor within sixty (60) days after the date the debt is
19 terminated, due to prepayment in full or otherwise, the creditor
20 shall pay to the debtor for each day after the sixty (60) day period
21 has expired an amount equal to the daily interest at the contracted
22 annual percentage rate on the amount of the refund required by
23 subsection (a) due at the time of prepayment or termination. The
24 director may impose an additional civil penalty of not greater than
25 one thousand dollars ($1,000) per occurrence if a creditor engages
26 in a pattern or practice of failing to comply with this subsection.
27 Sec. 7. If a creditor requires insurance, upon notice to the
28 creditor the debtor shall have the option of providing the required
29 insurance through an existing policy of insurance owned or
30 controlled by the debtor, or through a policy to be obtained and
31 paid for by the debtor, but the creditor may for reasonable cause
32 decline the insurance provided by the debtor.
33 Sec. 8. (a) A creditor may not contract for or receive a separate
34 charge for insurance in connection with a deferral (IC 37-2-3-7 or
35 IC 37-2-4-7), a refinancing (IC 37-2-3-8 or IC 37-2-4-8), or a
36 consolidation (IC 37-2-3-9 or IC 37-2-4-9), unless:
37 (1) the debtor agrees at or before the time of the deferral,
38 refinancing, or consolidation that the charge may be made;
39 (2) the debtor is or is to be provided with insurance for an
40 amount or a term, or insurance of a kind, in addition to that
41 to which the debtor would have been entitled had there been
42 no deferral, refinancing, or consolidation;
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

235
1 (3) the debtor receives a refund or credit on account of any
2 unexpired term of existing insurance in the amount that
3 would be required if the insurance were terminated under
4 section 6 of this chapter; and
5 (4) the charge does not exceed the amount permitted by
6 section 5 of this chapter.
7 (b) A creditor may not contract for or receive a separate charge
8 for insurance which duplicates insurance with respect to which the
9 creditor has previously contracted for or received a separate
10 charge.
11 Sec. 9. The department and the insurance commissioner are
12 authorized and directed to consult and assist one another in
13 maintaining compliance with this chapter. They may jointly pursue
14 investigations, prosecute suits, and take other official action, as
15 may seem to them appropriate, if either of them is otherwise
16 empowered to take the action. If the department is informed of a
17 violation or suspected violation by an insurer of this chapter, or of
18 the insurance laws, rules, and regulations of Indiana, the
19 department shall advise the insurance commissioner of the
20 circumstances.
21 Sec. 10. (a) To the extent that the commissioner's responsibility
22 under this chapter requires, the commissioner of insurance shall
23 issue rules with respect to insurers, and with respect to refunds
24 (section 6 of this chapter), forms, schedules of premium rates and
25 charges (section 13 of this chapter), and the commissioner's
26 approval or disapproval thereof and, in case of violation, may
27 make an order for compliance.
28 (b) IC 4-21.5-3 applies to and governs all agency action taken by
29 the commissioner of insurance pursuant to this section.
30 Sec. 11. (a) Consumer credit insurance provided by a creditor
31 may be subject to the furnishing of evidence of insurability
32 satisfactory to the insurer. Whether or not such evidence is
33 required, the term of the insurance shall commence no later than
34 when the debtor becomes obligated to the creditor or when the
35 debtor applies for the insurance, whichever is later, except as
36 follows:
37 (1) if any required evidence of insurability is not furnished
38 until more than thirty (30) days after the term would
39 otherwise commence, the term may commence on the date
40 when the insurer determines the evidence to be satisfactory;
41 or
42 (2) if the creditor provides insurance not previously provided
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

236
1 covering debts previously created, the term may commence on
2 the effective date of the policy.
3 (b) The originally scheduled term of the insurance shall extend
4 at least until the due date of the last scheduled payment of the debt
5 except as follows:
6 (1) if the insurance relates to a revolving charge account or
7 revolving loan account, the term need extend only until the
8 payment of the debt under the account and may be sooner
9 terminated after at least thirty (30) days notice to the debtor;
10 or
11 (2) if the debtor is advised in writing that the insurance will be
12 written for a specified shorter time, the term need extend only
13 until the end of the specified time.
14 (c) The term of the insurance shall not extend more than fifteen
15 (15) days after the originally scheduled due date of the last
16 scheduled payment of the debt unless it is extended without
17 additional cost to the debtor or as an incident to a deferral;
18 refinancing, or consolidation.
19 Sec. 12. (a) Except as provided in subsection (b):
20 (1) in the case of consumer credit insurance providing life
21 coverage, the amount of insurance may not initially exceed the
22 debt and, if the debt is payable in installments, may not at any
23 time exceed the greater of the scheduled or actual amount of
24 the debt; or
25 (2) in the case of any other consumer credit insurance, the
26 total amount of periodic benefits payable may not exceed the
27 total of scheduled unpaid installments of the debt, and the
28 amount of any periodic benefit may not exceed the original
29 amount of debt divided by the number of periodic
30 installments in which it is payable.
31 (b) If consumer credit insurance is provided in connection with
32 a revolving charge account or revolving loan account, the amounts
33 payable as insurance benefits may be reasonably commensurate
34 with the amount of debt as it exists from time to time. If consumer
35 credit insurance is provided in connection with a commitment to
36 grant credit in the future, the amounts payable as insurance
37 benefits may be reasonably commensurate with the total from time
38 to time of the amount of debt and the amount of the commitment.
39 Sec. 13. (a) A creditor may not use a form, or a schedule of
40 premium rates or charges, the filing of which is required by this
41 section, if the insurance commissioner has disapproved the form or
42 schedule and has notified the insurer of the insurance
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

237
1 commissioner's disapproval. A creditor may not use a form or
2 schedule unless:
3 (1) the form or schedule has been on file with the insurance
4 commissioner for thirty (30) days, or has earlier been
5 approved by the commissioner; and
6 (2) the insurer has complied with this section with respect to
7 the insurance.
8 (b) Except as provided in subsection (c), all policies, certificates
9 of insurance, notices of proposed insurance, applications for
10 insurance, endorsements and riders relating to consumer credit
11 insurance delivered or issued for delivery in this state, and the
12 schedules of premium rates or charges pertaining thereto, shall be
13 filed by the insurer with the insurance commissioner. Within thirty
14 (30) days after the filing of any form or schedule, the commissioner
15 shall disapprove it if the premium rates or charges are
16 unreasonable in relation to the benefits provided under the form,
17 or if the form contains provisions which are unjust, unfair,
18 inequitable, or deceptive, or encourage misrepresentation of the
19 coverage, or are contrary to any provision of IC 27 or of any rule
20 promulgated under IC 27.
21 (c) If a group policy has been delivered in another state, the
22 forms to be filed by the insurer with the insurance commissioner
23 are the group certificates and notices of proposed insurance. The
24 commissioner shall approve them if:
25 (1) they provide the information that would be required if the
26 group policy were delivered in this state; and
27 (2) the applicable premium rates or charges do not exceed
28 those established by the commissioner's rules.
29 Sec. 14. (a) A creditor may not contract for or receive a separate
30 charge for insurance against loss of or damage to property unless:
31 (1) the insurance covers a substantial risk of loss of or damage
32 to property related to the credit transaction;
33 (2) the amount, terms, and conditions of the insurance are
34 reasonable in relation to the character and value of the
35 property insured or to be insured; and
36 (3) the term of the insurance is reasonable in relation to the
37 terms of credit.
38 (b) The term of the insurance is reasonable if it is customary
39 and does not extend substantially beyond a scheduled maturity.
40 (c) A creditor may not contract for or receive a separate charge
41 for insurance against loss of or damage to property unless the
42 amount financed or principal exclusive of charges for the insurance
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

238
1 is three hundred dollars ($300) or more, and the value of the
2 property is three hundred dollars ($300) or more.
3 (d) The amounts of three hundred dollars ($300) in subsection
4 (c) are subject to change pursuant to the provisions on adjustment
5 of dollar amounts (IC 37-2-1-5). However, notwithstanding
6 IC 37-2-1-5(a), the Reference Base Index to be used under this
7 subsection is the Index for October 1992.
8 Sec. 15. If a creditor contracts for or receives a separate charge
9 for insurance against loss of or damage to property, the risk of loss
10 or damage not willfully caused by the debtor is on the debtor only
11 to the extent of any deficiency in the effective coverage of the
12 insurance, even though the insurance covers only the interest of the
13 creditor.
14 Sec. 16. A creditor may not contract for or receive a separate
15 charge for insurance against liability unless the insurance covers
16 a substantial risk of liability arising out of the ownership or use of
17 property related to the credit transaction.
18 Sec. 17. A creditor shall not request cancellation of a policy of
19 property or liability insurance except after the debtor's default or
20 in accordance with a written authorization by the debtor, and in
21 either case the cancellation does not take effect until written notice
22 is delivered to the debtor or mailed to the debtor at the debtor's
23 address as stated by the debtor. The notice shall state that the
24 policy may be canceled on a date not less than ten (10) days after
25 the notice is delivered, or, if the notice is mailed, not less than
26 thirteen (13) days after it is mailed.
27 Sec. 18. Upon the payment in full of a consumer credit sale or
28 consumer loan, the creditor or creditor's assignee shall promptly
29 make an appropriate refund of the unearned premium for any
30 property insurance with respect to which the creditor or the credit
31 account of the consumer is a beneficiary.
32 Chapter 6. Remedies and Penalties
33 Sec. 0.1. The amendments made to IC 24-4.5-5-203 (before its
34 repeal) by P.L.181-1991 applied to causes of action accruing after
35 June 30, 1991.
36 Sec. 1. Sections 0.1 through 7 of this chapter apply to:
37 (1) actions or other proceedings to enforce rights arising from
38 consumer credit sales, consumer leases, and consumer loans;
39 (2) garnishments of the earnings of an individual; and
40 (3) extortionate extensions of credit.
41 Sec. 2. (a) This section applies to a consumer credit sale of goods
42 or services.
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

239
1 (b) If the seller repossesses or voluntarily accepts surrender of
2 goods which were the subject of the sale and in which the seller has
3 a security interest, and the cash price of the goods repossessed or
4 surrendered was four thousand dollars ($4,000) or less, the buyer
5 is not personally liable to the seller for the unpaid balance of the
6 debt arising from the sale of the goods, and the seller is not
7 obligated to resell the collateral.
8 (c) If the seller repossesses or voluntarily accepts surrender of
9 goods which were not the subject of the sale but in which the seller
10 has a security interest to secure a debt arising from a sale of goods
11 or services or a combined sale of goods and services and the cash
12 price of the sale was four thousand dollars ($4,000) or less, the
13 buyer is not personally liable to the seller for the unpaid balance of
14 the debt arising from the sale.
15 (d) For the purpose of determining the unpaid balance of
16 consolidated debts or debts pursuant to revolving charge accounts,
17 the allocation of payments to a debt shall be determined in the
18 same manner as provided for determining the amount of debt
19 secured by various security interests under IC 37-2-3-20.
20 (e) The buyer may be liable in damages to the seller if the buyer
21 has wrongfully damaged the collateral or if, after default and
22 demand, the buyer has wrongfully failed to make the collateral
23 available to the seller.
24 (f) If the seller elects to bring an action against the buyer for a
25 debt arising from a consumer credit sale of goods or services, and
26 under this section the seller would not be entitled to a deficiency
27 judgment if the seller repossessed the collateral, and the seller
28 obtains a judgment:
29 (1) the seller may not repossess the collateral; and
30 (2) the collateral is not subject to levy or sale on execution or
31 similar proceedings pursuant to the judgment.
32 (g) The amounts of four thousand dollars ($4,000) in subsections
33 (b) and (c) are subject to change pursuant to the provisions on
34 adjustment of dollar amounts (IC 37-2-1-5). However,
35 notwithstanding IC 37-2-1-5(a), the Reference Base Index to be
36 used under this subsection is the Index for October 2012.
37 Sec. 3. Prior to entry of judgment in an action against the
38 debtor, no creditor may attach unpaid earnings of the debtor by
39 garnishment or like proceedings.
40 Sec. 4. (a) For the purposes of sections 1 through 7 of this
41 chapter:
42 (1) "disposable earnings" means that part of the earnings of
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

240
1 an individual, including wages, commissions, income, rents, or
2 profits remaining after the deduction from those earnings of
3 amounts required by law to be withheld;
4 (2) "garnishment" means any legal or equitable proceedings
5 through which the earnings of an individual are required to
6 be withheld by a garnishee, by the individual debtor, or by
7 any other person for the payment of a judgment; and
8 (3) "support withholding" means that part of the earnings
9 that are withheld from an individual for child support in
10 accordance with the laws of this state.
11 (b) Except as provided in subsection (h), the maximum part of
12 the aggregate disposable earnings of an individual for any
13 workweek which is subjected to garnishment to enforce the
14 payment of one (1) or more judgments against the individual may
15 not exceed the lesser of the following amounts:
16 (1) An amount equal to twenty-five percent (25%) of the
17 individual's disposable earnings for that week or, upon a
18 showing of good cause by the individual why the amount
19 should be reduced, an amount equal to:
20 (A) less than twenty-five percent (25%); and
21 (B) at least ten percent (10%);
22 of the individual's disposable earnings for that week.
23 (2) The amount by which the individual's disposable earnings
24 for that week exceed thirty (30) times the federal minimum
25 hourly wage prescribed by 29 U.S.C. 206(a)(1) in effect at the
26 time the earnings are payable.
27 In the case of earnings for a pay period other than a week, the
28 earnings shall be computed upon a multiple of the federal
29 minimum hourly wage equivalent to thirty (30) times the federal
30 minimum hourly wage as prescribed in this section.
31 (c) The maximum part of the aggregate disposable earnings of
32 an individual for any workweek which is subject to garnishment or
33 support withholding to enforce any order for the support of any
34 person shall not exceed:
35 (1) where such individual is supporting the individual's spouse
36 or dependent child (other than a spouse or child with respect
37 to whose support such order is used), fifty percent (50%) of
38 such individual's disposable earnings for that week; and
39 (2) where such individual is not supporting such a spouse or
40 dependent child described in subdivision (1), sixty percent
41 (60%) of such individual's disposable earnings for that week;
42 except that, with respect to the disposable earnings of any
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

241
1 individual for any workweek, the fifty percent (50%) specified in
2 subdivision (1) shall be deemed to be fifty-five percent (55%) and
3 the sixty percent (60%) specified in subdivision (2) shall be deemed
4 to be sixty-five percent (65%), if and to the extent that such
5 earnings are subject to garnishment or support withholding to
6 enforce a support order with respect to a period which is prior to
7 the twelve (12) week period which ends with the beginning of such
8 workweek.
9 (d) No court may make, execute, or enforce an order or process
10 in violation of this section.
11 (e) An employer who is required to make deductions from an
12 individual's disposable earnings pursuant to a garnishment order
13 or series of orders arising out of the same judgment debt
14 (excluding a judgment for payment of child support) may collect,
15 as a fee to compensate the employer for making these deductions,
16 an amount equal to the greater of twelve dollars ($12) or three
17 percent (3%) of the total amount required to be deducted by the
18 garnishment order or series of orders arising out of the same
19 judgment debt. If the employer chooses to impose a fee, the fee
20 shall be allocated as follows:
21 (1) One-half (1/2) of the fee shall be borne by the debtor, and
22 that amount may be deducted by the employer directly from
23 the employee's disposable earnings.
24 (2) One-half (1/2) of the fee shall be borne by the creditor, and
25 that amount may be retained by the employer from the
26 amount otherwise due the creditor.
27 The deductions made under this subsection for a collection fee do
28 not increase the amount of the judgment debt for which the fee is
29 collected for the purpose of calculating or collecting judgment
30 interest. This fee may be collected by an employer only once for
31 each garnishment order or series of orders arising out of the same
32 judgment debt. The employer may collect the entire fee from one
33 (1) or more of the initial deductions from the employee's disposable
34 earnings. Alternatively, the employer may collect the fee ratably
35 over the number of pay periods during which deductions from the
36 employee's disposable earnings are required.
37 (f) The deduction of the garnishment collection fee under
38 subsection (e)(1) or subsection (g) is not an assignment of wages
39 under IC 22-2-6.
40 (g) An employer who is required to make a deduction from an
41 individual's disposable earnings in accordance with a judgment for
42 payment of child support may collect a fee of two dollars ($2) each
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

242
1 time the employer is required to make the deduction. The fee may
2 be deducted by the employer from the individual's disposable
3 earnings each time the employer makes the deduction for support.
4 If the employer elects to deduct such a fee, the amount to be
5 deducted for the payment of support must be reduced accordingly
6 if necessary to avoid exceeding the maximum amount permitted to
7 be deducted under subsection (c).
8 (h) A support withholding order takes priority over a
9 garnishment order irrespective of their dates of entry or activation.
10 If a person is subject to a support withholding order and a
11 garnishment order, the garnishment order shall be honored only
12 to the extent that disposable earnings withheld under the support
13 withholding order do not exceed the maximum amount subject to
14 garnishment as computed under subsection (b).
15 Sec. 5. No employer shall discharge an employee for the reason
16 that a creditor or creditors of the employee has subjected or
17 attempted to subject unpaid earnings of the employee to
18 garnishment or like proceedings directed to the employer for the
19 purpose of paying a judgment or judgments.
20 Sec. 6. (a) If it is the understanding of the creditor and the
21 debtor at the time an extension of credit is made that delay in
22 making repayment or failure to make repayment could result in
23 the use of violence or other criminal means to cause harm to the
24 person, reputation, or property of any person, the repayment of the
25 extension of credit is unenforceable through civil judicial processes
26 against the debtor.
27 (b) If it is shown that an extension of credit was made at an
28 annual rate exceeding forty-five percent (45%) calculated
29 according to the actuarial method and that the creditor then had
30 a reputation for the use or threat of use of violence or other
31 criminal means to cause harm to the person, reputation, or
32 property of any person to collect extensions of credit or to punish
33 the nonrepayment thereof, there is prima facie evidence that the
34 extension of credit was unenforceable under subsection (a).
35 Sec. 7. (a) With respect to a consumer credit sale, consumer
36 lease, or consumer loan, if the court as a matter of law finds the
37 agreement or any clause of the agreement to have been
38 unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to
39 enforce the agreement, or it may enforce the remainder of the
40 agreement without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the
41 application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any
42 unconscionable result.
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

243
1 (b) If it is claimed or appears to the court that the agreement or
2 any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be
3 afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its
4 setting, purpose, and effect to aid the court in making the
5 determination.
6 (c) For the purpose of this section, a charge or practice
7 expressly permitted by this article is not in itself unconscionable.
8 Sec. 8. For purposes of the provisions on civil liability for
9 violation of disclosure provisions set forth in section 10 of this
10 chapter and on a debtor's right to rescind certain transactions set
11 forth in section 11 of this chapter a:
12 (1) consumer credit sale includes a sale that is a first lien
13 mortgage transaction if the sale is otherwise a consumer
14 credit sale; and
15 (2) consumer loan includes a loan that is a first lien mortgage
16 transaction if the loan is otherwise a consumer loan.
17 Sec. 9. (a) If a creditor has violated the provision of this article
18 applying to limitations on the schedule of payments or loan term
19 for supervised loans (IC 37-2-4-38), the debtor is not obligated to
20 pay the loan finance charge, and has a right to recover from the
21 person violating this article or from an assignee of that person's
22 rights who undertakes direct collection of payments or
23 enforcement of rights arising from the debt a penalty in an amount
24 determined by the court not in excess of three times the amount of
25 the loan finance charge. No action pursuant to this subsection may
26 be brought more than one (1) year after the due date of the last
27 scheduled payment of the agreement with respect to which the
28 violation occurred.
29 (b) If a creditor has violated the provisions of this article
30 applying to authority to make consumer loans (IC 37-2-4-23), the
31 loan is void and the debtor is not obligated to pay either the
32 principal or loan finance charge. If the debtor has paid any part of
33 the principal or of the loan finance charge, the debtor has a right
34 to recover the payment from the person violating this article or
35 from an assignee of that person's rights who undertakes direct
36 collection of payments or enforcement of rights arising from the
37 debt. With respect to violations arising from loans made pursuant
38 to revolving loan accounts, no action pursuant to this subsection
39 may be brought more than two (2) years after the violation
40 occurred. With respect to violations arising from other loans, no
41 action pursuant to this subsection may be brought more than one
42 (1) year after the due date of the last scheduled payment of the
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

244
1 agreement pursuant to which the charge was paid.
2 (c) A debtor is not obligated to pay a charge in excess of that
3 allowed by this article, and if the debtor has paid an excess charge
4 the debtor has a right to a refund. A refund may be made by
5 reducing the debtor's obligation by the amount of the excess
6 charge. If the debtor has paid an amount in excess of the lawful
7 obligation under the agreement, the debtor may recover the excess
8 amount from the person who made the excess charge or from an
9 assignee of that person's rights who undertakes direct collection of
10 payments from or enforcement of rights against debtors arising
11 from the debt.
12 (d) If a debtor is entitled to a refund and a person liable to the
13 debtor refuses to make a refund within a reasonable time after
14 demand, the debtor may recover from that person a penalty in an
15 amount determined by a court not exceeding the greater of either
16 the amount of the credit service or loan finance charge or ten (10)
17 times the amount of the excess charge. If the creditor has made an
18 excess charge in deliberate violation of or in reckless disregard for
19 this article, the penalty may be recovered even though the creditor
20 has refunded the excess charge. No penalty pursuant to this
21 subsection may be recovered if a court has ordered a similar
22 penalty assessed against the same person in a civil action by the
23 department (IC 37-2-7-14). With respect to excess charges arising
24 from sales made pursuant to revolving charge accounts or from
25 loans made pursuant to revolving loan accounts, no action
26 pursuant to this subsection may be brought more than two (2)
27 years after the time the excess charge was made. With respect to
28 excess charges arising from other consumer credit sales or
29 consumer loans, no action pursuant to this subsection may be
30 brought more than one (1) year after the due date of the last
31 scheduled payment of the agreement pursuant to which the charge
32 was made.
33 (e) Except as otherwise provided, no violation of this article
34 impairs rights on a debt.
35 (f) If an employer discharges an employee in violation of the
36 provisions prohibiting discharge (section 5 of this chapter), the
37 employee may within six (6) months bring a civil action for
38 recovery of wages lost as a result of the violation and for an order
39 requiring the reinstatement of the employee. Damages recoverable
40 shall not exceed lost wages for six (6) weeks.
41 (g) If the creditor establishes by a preponderance of evidence
42 that a violation is unintentional or the result of a bona fide error,
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

245
1 no liability is imposed under subsections (a), (b), and (d) and the
2 validity of the transaction is not affected.
3 (h) In any case in which it is found that a creditor has violated
4 this article, the court may award reasonable attorney's fees
5 incurred by the debtor.
6 (i) The department may act on behalf of a debtor to enforce the
7 debtor's rights under this section against a creditor who is licensed
8 or registered with the department or is required to be licensed or
9 registered with the department.
10 Sec. 10. (a) Except as otherwise provided in this section, a
11 creditor who, in violation of the provisions on disclosure in
12 IC 37-2-3-13 and IC 37-2-4-14, fails to disclose information to a
13 person entitled to the information under this article is liable to that
14 person in an amount equal to the sum of:
15 (1) the following:
16 (A) In the case of an individual action, twice the amount of
17 the credit service or loan finance charge in connection with
18 the transaction, but the liability pursuant to this
19 subdivision shall be not less than one hundred dollars
20 ($100) nor more than one thousand dollars ($1,000).
21 (B) In the case of a class action, an amount the court
22 allows, except that as to each member of the class no
23 minimum recovery is applicable, and the total recovery
24 under this subdivision in any class action or series of class
25 actions arising out of the same failure to comply by the
26 same creditor may not be more than the lesser of:
27 (i) five hundred thousand dollars ($500,000); or
28 (ii) one percent (1%) of the net worth of the creditor.
29 (2) In the case of a successful action to enforce the liability
30 under subdivision (1), the costs of the action together with
31 reasonable attorney's fees as determined by the court. In
32 determining the amount of the award in a class action, the
33 court shall consider, among other relevant factors, the
34 amount of any award granted under the Consumer Credit
35 Protection Act (15 U.S.C. 1601 et seq.), the frequency and
36 persistence of failures of compliance by the creditor, the
37 resources of the creditor, the number of persons adversely
38 affected, and the extent to which the creditor's failure of
39 compliance was intentional.
40 (b) A creditor has no liability under this section if within sixty
41 (60) days after discovering an error, and prior to the institution of
42 an action under this section or the receipt of written notice of the
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

246
1 error, the creditor notifies the person concerned of the error and
2 makes whatever adjustments in the appropriate account are
3 necessary to assure that the person will not be required to pay a
4 credit service charge or loan finance charge in excess of the
5 amount or percentage rate actually disclosed.
6 (c) A creditor may not be held liable in any action brought
7 under this section for a violation of this article if the creditor shows
8 by a preponderance of evidence that the violation was not
9 intentional and resulted from a bona fide error notwithstanding
10 the maintenance of procedures reasonably adapted to avoid the
11 error.
12 (d) If there are multiple obligors in a consumer credit
13 transaction or consumer lease, there may not be more than one (1)
14 recovery of damages under subsection (a)(1) for one (1) violation
15 of this article with respect to that consumer credit transaction or
16 consumer lease.
17 (e) The multiple failure to disclose to any person any
18 information required under this article to be disclosed in
19 connection with a single account under an open end consumer
20 credit plan, a single consumer credit sale, a consumer loan, a
21 consumer lease, or another extension of consumer credit entitles
22 that person to a single recovery under this section. However,
23 continued failure to disclose after a recovery has been granted
24 gives rise to rights to additional recoveries.
25 (f) Any action which may be brought under this section against
26 the original creditor in any credit transaction involving a security
27 interest in land may be maintained against any subsequent assignee
28 of the original creditor where the assignee, its subsidiaries, or
29 affiliates were in a continuing business relationship with the
30 original creditor either at the time the credit was extended or at the
31 time of the assignment, unless the assignment was involuntary, or
32 the assignee shows by a preponderance of evidence that it did not
33 have reasonable grounds to believe that the original creditor was
34 engaged in violations of this article, and that it maintained
35 procedures reasonably adapted to apprise it of the existence of the
36 violations.
37 (g) No action pursuant to this section may be brought more than
38 one (1) year after the date of the occurrence of the violations.
39 (h) In this section, creditor includes a person who in the
40 ordinary course of business regularly extends or arranges for the
41 extension of credit, or offers to arrange for the extension of credit.
42 Sec. 11. (a) A violation by a creditor of Section 125 of the
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

247
1 Consumer Credit Protection Act concerning the debtor's right to
2 rescind a transaction that is a consumer credit sale or a consumer
3 loan constitutes a violation of this article. A creditor may not
4 accrue interest during the period when a consumer loan may be
5 rescinded under Section 125 of the Consumer Credit Protection
6 Act (15 U.S.C. 1635).
7 (b) A creditor must make available for disbursement the
8 proceeds of a transaction subject to subsection (a) on the later of:
9 (1) the date the creditor is reasonably satisfied that the
10 consumer has not rescinded the transaction; or
11 (2) the first business day after the expiration of the rescission
12 period under subsection (a).
13 Sec. 12. Refunds or penalties to which the debtor is entitled
14 pursuant to sections 8 through 11 of this chapter may be set off
15 against the debtor's obligation, and may be raised as a defense to
16 a suit on the obligation without regard to the time limitations
17 prescribed by sections 8 through 11 of this chapter.
18 Sec. 13. (a) A lender who knowingly makes charges in excess of
19 those permitted by the provisions of this article commits a Class A
20 misdemeanor.
21 (b) A person who knowingly engages in the business of making
22 consumer loans without a license in violation of the provisions of
23 this article applying to authority to make consumer loans (IC
24 37-2-4-23 and IC 37-2-4-24) commits a Class A misdemeanor.
25 (c) A person who knowingly:
26 (1) engages in the business of making consumer credit sales,
27 consumer leases, or consumer loans, or of taking assignments
28 of rights against debtors; and
29 (2) undertakes direct collection of payments or enforcement
30 of these rights, without complying with the provisions of this
31 article concerning notification (IC 37-2-7-25) or payment of
32 fees (IC 37-2-7-26);
33 commits a Class A infraction.
34 Sec. 14. A person commits a Class A misdemeanor if the person
35 knowingly gives false or inaccurate information or fails to provide
36 information which the person is required to disclose under the
37 provisions of IC 37-2-3-13 or IC 37-2-4-14.
38 Chapter 7. Administration
39 Sec. 1. (a) Sections 1 through 16 of this chapter apply to persons
40 who:
41 (1) make or solicit consumer credit sales, consumer leases,
42 consumer loans, consumer related sales (as described in
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

248
1 IC 37-2-2-13.5), and consumer related loans (as described in
2 IC 37-2-2-13.3); or
3 (2) directly collect payments from or enforce rights against
4 debtors arising from sales, leases, or loans specified in
5 subsection (a), wherever they are made.
6 (b) For purposes of sections 1 through 16 of this chapter:
7 (1) "consumer credit sale" includes a sale that is a first lien
8 mortgage transaction if the sale is otherwise a consumer
9 credit sale; and
10 (2) "consumer loan" includes a loan that is a first lien
11 mortgage transaction if the loan is otherwise a consumer loan.
12 Sec. 2. The division of consumer credit shall have charge of the
13 administration of this article.
14 Sec. 3. (a) In addition to other powers granted by this article,
15 the department within the limitations provided by law may:
16 (1) receive and act on complaints, take action designed to
17 obtain voluntary compliance with this article, or commence
18 proceedings on the department's own initiative;
19 (2) counsel persons and groups on their rights and duties
20 under this article;
21 (3) establish programs for the education of consumers with
22 respect to credit practices and problems;
23 (4) make studies appropriate to effectuate the purposes and
24 policies of this article and make the results available to the
25 public;
26 (5) adopt, amend, and repeal rules, orders, policies, and forms
27 to carry out the provisions of this article;
28 (6) maintain more than one (1) office within Indiana; and
29 (7) appoint any necessary attorneys, hearing examiners,
30 clerks, and other employees and agents and fix their
31 compensation, and authorize attorneys appointed under this
32 section to appear for and represent the department in court.
33 (b) No liability is imposed under this article for an act done or
34 omitted in conformity with a rule, written notice, written opinion,
35 written interpretation, or written directive of the department
36 notwithstanding that after the act or omission the rule, written
37 notice, written opinion, written interpretation, or written directive
38 may be amended or repealed, or be determined by judicial or other
39 authority to be invalid for any reason.
40 Sec. 4. (a) With respect to depository institutions, the powers of
41 examination and investigation (section 5 of this chapter) and
42 administrative enforcement (section 9 of this chapter) shall be
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

249
1 exercised by the department. The department may, at its
2 discretion, accept any examination of any financial institution
3 made by a federal authority in lieu of the examination made under
4 the provisions of this article. All other powers of the department
5 under this article may be exercised by the director with respect to
6 a depository institution.
7 (b) If the department receives a complaint or other information
8 concerning noncompliance with this article by a depository
9 institution, the director shall inform the official or agency having
10 supervisory authority over the organization concerned. The
11 department may request information about depository institutions
12 from the officials or agencies supervising them.
13 (c) The department and any official or agency of this state
14 having supervisory authority over a depository institution are
15 authorized and directed to consult and assist one another in
16 maintaining compliance with this article. They may jointly pursue
17 investigations, prosecute suits, and take other official action, as
18 they deem appropriate, if either of them otherwise is empowered
19 to take the action.
20 Sec. 5. (a) In administering this article and in order to determine
21 whether the provisions of this article are being complied with by
22 persons engaging in acts subject to this article, the department may
23 examine the records of persons and may make investigations of
24 persons as may be necessary to determine compliance. Records
25 subject to examination under this section include the following:
26 (1) Training, operating, and policy manuals.
27 (2) Minutes of:
28 (A) management meetings; and
29 (B) other meetings.
30 (3) Other records that the department determines are
31 necessary to perform its investigation or examination.
32 The department may also administer oaths or affirmations,
33 subpoena witnesses, and compel the attendance of witnesses,
34 including directors, executive officers, managers, principals,
35 mortgage loan originators, employees, independent contractors,
36 agents, and customers of the licensee, individual, or person subject
37 to this article. The department may also adduce evidence, and
38 require the production of any matter which is relevant to the
39 investigation. The department shall determine the sufficiency of the
40 records maintained and whether the person has made the required
41 information reasonably available. The records pertaining to any
42 transaction subject to this article shall be retained for two (2) years
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

250
1 after making the final entry relating to the consumer credit
2 transaction, but in the case of a revolving loan account or revolving
3 charge account, the two (2) years is measured from the date of each
4 entry.
5 (b) The department's examination and investigatory authority
6 under this article includes the following:
7 (1) The authority to require a creditor to refund overcharges
8 resulting from the creditor's noncompliance with the terms of
9 consumer credit sales, consumer leases, or consumer loans.
10 (2) The authority to require a creditor to comply with the
11 prepayment penalty provisions set forth in IC 37-2-4-12.
12 (3) The authority to investigate complaints filed with the
13 department by debtors.
14 (c) If the department:
15 (1) investigates; or
16 (2) examines the books and records of;
17 a person that is subject to sections 24 through 26 of this chapter,
18 the person shall pay all reasonably incurred costs of the
19 investigation or examination in accordance with the fee schedule
20 adopted by the department under IC 28-11-3-5. However, the
21 person is liable for the costs of an investigation or examination
22 under this subsection only to the extent that the costs exceed the
23 amount of the filing fees paid most recently under section 26 of this
24 chapter. Any costs required to be paid under this section shall be
25 paid not later than sixty (60) days after the person receives a notice
26 from the department of the costs being assessed. The department
27 may impose a fee, in an amount fixed by the department under
28 IC 28-11-3-5, for each day that the assessed costs are not paid,
29 beginning on the first day after the sixty (60) day period described
30 in this subsection.
31 (d) The department shall be given free access to the records
32 wherever located. In making any examination or investigation
33 authorized by this article, the director may control access to any
34 documents and records of the licensee or person under
35 examination or investigation. The director may take possession of
36 the documents and records or place a person in exclusive charge of
37 the documents and records in the place where the documents are
38 usually kept. During the period of control, the licensee or person
39 may not remove or attempt to remove any of the documents and
40 records except under a court order or with the consent of the
41 director. Unless the director has reasonable grounds to believe the
42 documents or records of the licensee or person have been, or are,
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

251
1 at risk of being altered or destroyed for purposes of concealing a
2 violation of this article, the licensee or person being examined or
3 investigated is entitled to access to the documents or records as
4 necessary to conduct the licensee's or person's ordinary business
5 affairs. If the person's records are located outside Indiana, the
6 records shall be made available to the department at a convenient
7 location within Indiana, or the person shall pay the reasonable and
8 necessary expenses for the department or its representative to
9 examine them where they are maintained. The department may
10 designate comparable officials of the state in which the records are
11 located to inspect them on behalf of the department.
12 (e) Upon a person's failure without lawful excuse to obey a
13 subpoena or to give testimony and upon reasonable notice to all
14 affected persons, the department may apply to any civil court with
15 jurisdiction for an order compelling compliance.
16 (f) The department shall not make public the name or identity
17 of a person whose acts or conduct the department investigates
18 pursuant to this section or the facts disclosed in the investigation,
19 but this subsection does not apply to disclosures in actions or
20 enforcement proceedings pursuant to this article.
21 (g) To discover violations of this article or to secure information
22 necessary for the enforcement of this article, the department may
23 investigate any:
24 (1) licensee or registrant; or
25 (2) person that the department suspects to be operating:
26 (A) without a license or registration, when a license or
27 registration is required under this article; or
28 (B) otherwise in violation of this article.
29 The department has all investigatory and enforcement authority
30 under this article that the department has under IC 28-11 with
31 respect to financial institutions. If the department conducts an
32 investigation under this section, the licensee, registrant, or other
33 person investigated shall pay all reasonably incurred costs of the
34 investigation in accordance with the fee schedule adopted under
35 IC 28-11-3-5. Any costs required to be paid under this section shall
36 be paid not later than sixty (60) days after the person receives a
37 notice from the department of the costs being assessed. The
38 department may impose a fee, in an amount fixed by the
39 department under IC 28-11-3-5, for each day that the assessed
40 costs are not paid, beginning on the first day after the sixty (60)
41 day period described in this subsection.
42 (h) If a creditor contracts with an outside vendor to provide a
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

252
1 service that would otherwise be undertaken internally by the
2 creditor and be subject to the department's routine examination
3 procedures, the person that provides the service to the creditor
4 shall, at the request of the director, submit to an examination by
5 the department. If the director determines that an examination
6 under this subsection is necessary or desirable, the examination
7 may be made at the expense of the person to be examined. If the
8 person to be examined under this subsection refuses to permit the
9 examination to be made, the director may order any creditor that
10 is licensed by the department and that receives services from the
11 person refusing the examination to:
12 (1) discontinue receiving one (1) or more services from the
13 person; or
14 (2) otherwise cease conducting business with the person.

23 (A) resources;
24 (B) standardized or uniform methods or procedures; and
25 (C) documents, records, information, or evidence obtained
26 under this section;
27 (3) use, hire, contract, or employ public or privately available

30 article;
31 (4) accept and rely on examination or investigation reports
32 made by other government officials, in or outside Indiana;

35 subject to this article in the course of that part of the
36 examination covering the same general subject matter as the

38 the examination, report of investigation, or other writing of
39 the director; and
40 (6) use, hire, contract, or employ public or privately available
41 analytical systems, methods, software, or technology solutions
42 to enable the department to capture and examine transaction

1 level activity to:
2 (A) verify and oversee compliance with; and
