8 ARTICLE 4. MORTGAGE RESCUE PROTECTION FRAUD
9 Chapter 1. Application
10 Sec. 1. This article does not apply to the following:
11 (1) A person organized or chartered under the laws of this
12 state, any other state, or the United States that relate to a
13 bank, a trust company, a savings association, a savings bank,
14 a credit union, or an industrial loan and investment company.
15 (2) The Federal National Mortgage Association, the Federal
16 Home Loan Mortgage Corporation, or a Federal Home Loan
17 Bank.
18 (3) A department or agency of the United States or of Indiana.
19 (4) A person that is servicing or enforcing a loan that it owns.
20 (5) A person that is servicing a loan:
21 (A) for a person described in subdivisions (1) through (4);
22 or
23 (B) insured by the Department of Housing and Urban
24 Development or guaranteed by the Veterans
25 Administration.
26 (6) An attorney licensed to practice law in Indiana who is
27 representing a mortgagor.
28 Chapter 2. Definitions
29 Sec. 1. The definitions in this chapter apply throughout this
30 article.
31 Sec. 2. "Foreclosure consultant" means a person who, directly
32 or indirectly, makes a solicitation, a representation, or an offer to
33 a homeowner to perform, with or without compensation, any
34 service that the person represents will:
35 (1) prevent or postpone the initiation of a foreclosure
36 proceeding, or reverse the effect of a foreclosure proceeding;
37 (2) allow the homeowner to become a lessee or renter in the
38 homeowner's residence during or after a foreclosure
39 proceeding; or
40 (3) allow the homeowner to have an option to repurchase the
41 homeowner's residence after a foreclosure proceeding.
42 Sec. 3. "Foreclosure purchaser" means a person who purchases
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

280
1 real property in a foreclosure proceeding.
2 Sec. 4. "Foreclosure reconveyance" means a transaction
3 involving:
4 (1) the transfer of interest in real property by a homeowner
5 to a person during or incident to a proposed foreclosure
6 proceeding, either by:
7 (A) transfer of interest from the homeowner to the person;
8 or
9 (B) creation of a mortgage, trust, or other lien or
10 encumbrance during the foreclosure process;
11 that allows the person to obtain legal or equitable title to all
12 or part of the real property; and
13 (2) the subsequent conveyance, or promise of subsequent
14 conveyance, of interest back to the homeowner by the person
15 or the person's agent that allows the homeowner to possess
16 the real property following the completion of the foreclosure
17 proceeding.
18 Sec. 5. "Formal settlement" means a face-to-face meeting with
19 a homeowner to complete final documents incident to the:
20 (1) sale or transfer of real property; or
21 (2) creation of a mortgage or equitable interest in real
22 property;
23 conducted by a person who is not employed by or an affiliate of the
24 foreclosure purchaser.
25 Sec. 6. "Homeowner" means a person who holds record title to
26 residential real property as of the date on which:
27 (1) a contract with a foreclosure consultant; or
28 (2) a foreclosure reconveyance agreement;
29 with respect to the residential real property is entered into.
30 Chapter 3. Rescission of Contracts With Foreclosure
31 Consultants and Foreclosure Reconveyance Agreements
32 Sec. 1. In addition to any other right under law to cancel or
33 rescind a contract, a homeowner may rescind a:
34 (1) contract with a foreclosure consultant at any time before
35 midnight of the seventh business day after the date the
36 contract is signed; and
37 (2) foreclosure reconveyance agreement at any time before
38 midnight of the seventh business day after the homeowner's
39 transfer of the interest in the real property that is the subject
40 of the agreement, as described in IC 37-4-2-4(1).
41 Sec. 2. A homeowner effectively rescinds a contract with a
42 foreclosure consultant if the homeowner gives written notice of a
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

281
1 rescission to the foreclosure consultant by one (1) of the following:
2 (1) Mailing the rescission to the address specified in the
3 contract.
4 (2) Sending the rescission through any facsimile or electronic
5 mail address identified in the contract or other material
6 provided to the homeowner by the foreclosure consultant.
7 Sec. 3. (a) If a notice of rescission under this chapter is sent by
8 mail, the rescission is effective three (3) days after the notice is
9 deposited in the United States mail, properly addressed, with
10 postage prepaid.
11 (b) A homeowner is not required to give notice of rescission in
12 the form required under the contract if the form under the
13 contract is inconsistent with the requirements under this chapter.
14 Sec. 4. (a) If a homeowner rescinds a contract with a foreclosure
15 consultant or a foreclosure reconveyance agreement, the
16 homeowner shall, not later than thirty (30) days after the date of
17 rescission, repay any amounts paid or advanced by:
18 (1) the foreclosure consultant or the foreclosure consultant's
19 agent under the terms of the foreclosure consulting contract;
20 or
21 (2) a person under a foreclosure reconveyance agreement.
22 (b) A rescission by a homeowner under this chapter is void if the
23 payments required under this section are not made within the time
24 set forth in subsection (a).
25 Sec. 5. If a homeowner rescinds a contract with a foreclosure
26 consultant, not less than ten (10) days following the effective date
27 of the rescission, the consultant shall return to the homeowner any
28 payments made by the homeowner, less any amounts for actual
29 services rendered.
30 Chapter 4. Limitations on Foreclosure Consultants and
31 Foreclosure Reconveyances
32 Sec. 1. For purposes of this chapter, there is a rebuttable
33 presumption that:
34 (1) a homeowner has a reasonable ability to pay for a
35 subsequent reconveyance of real property if the homeowner's
36 payments for primary housing expenses and regular principal
37 and interest payments on other personal debt, on a monthly
38 basis, do not exceed sixty percent (60%) of the homeowner's
39 monthly gross income; and
40 (2) the foreclosure purchaser has not verified reasonable
41 payment ability if the foreclosure purchaser has not obtained
42 documents other than a statement by the homeowner of
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

282
1 assets, liability, and income.
2 Sec. 2. In addition to any prohibitions that apply under
3 IC 24-5-15-1 through IC 24-5-15-8, a foreclosure consultant may
4 not:
5 (1) enter into or attempt to enter into a foreclosure consultant
6 contract with a homeowner unless the foreclosure consultant
7 first provides the homeowner written notice of the
8 homeowner's rights under this article;
9 (2) demand or receive compensation until after the
10 foreclosure consultant has fully performed all services the
11 foreclosure consultant contracted to perform or represented
12 that the foreclosure consultant would perform, unless the
13 foreclosure consultant complies with the security
14 requirements under IC 24-5-15-8;
15 (3) demand or receive a fee, interest, or any other
16 compensation that exceeds eight percent (8%) per year of the
17 amount of any loan that the foreclosure consultant makes to
18 the homeowner;
19 (4) take a wage assignment, a lien of any type on real or
20 personal property, or any other security to secure the
21 payment of compensation;
22 (5) receive consideration from a third party in connection
23 with foreclosure consulting services provided to a homeowner
24 unless the consideration is first fully disclosed in writing to the
25 homeowner;
26 (6) acquire any interest, directly or indirectly, in residential
27 real property in foreclosure from a homeowner with whom
28 the foreclosure consultant has contracted;
29 (7) except to inspect documents as provided by law, take any
30 power of attorney from a homeowner for any purpose;
31 (8) execute any contract or agreement with a homeowner or
32 receive money or other valuable consideration from a
33 homeowner without providing the homeowner with the
34 written statement required by IC 24-5-15-6; or
35 (9) fail to provide a homeowner with a written contract that
36 includes the notice of cancellation required by IC 24-5-15-7.
37 Sec. 3. A foreclosure purchaser may not enter into or attempt to
38 enter into a foreclosure reconveyance agreement with a
39 homeowner unless the:
40 (1) foreclosure purchaser verifies and demonstrates that the
41 homeowner has or will have a reasonable ability to:
42 (A) pay for the subsequent reconveyance of the property
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

283
1 back to the homeowner on completion of the terms of the
2 foreclosure conveyance; or
3 (B) if the foreclosure conveyance provides for a lease with
4 an option to repurchase the real property, make the lease
5 payment and repurchase the real property within the
6 period of the option to repurchase;
7 (2) foreclosure purchaser provides the homeowner written
8 notice of the homeowner's rights under this article;
9 (3) foreclosure purchaser and the homeowner complete a
10 formal settlement before any transfer of interest in the
11 affected property; and
12 (4) foreclosure purchaser complies with the security
13 requirements under IC 24-5-15-8.
14 Sec. 4. A foreclosure purchaser shall:
15 (1) ensure that title to real property has been reconveyed to
16 the homeowner in a timely manner if the terms of a
17 foreclosure reconveyance agreement require a reconveyance;
18 or
19 (2) if the real property subject to a foreclosure reconveyance
20 agreement is sold within eighteen (18) months after entering
21 into the foreclosure reconveyance agreement, make payment
22 to the homeowner not later than ninety (90) days after the
23 resale of the real property in an amount equal to at least
24 sixty-six percent (66%) of the net proceeds from the resale of
25 the property.
26 Sec. 5. A foreclosure purchaser may not:
27 (1) enter into repurchase or lease terms as part of the
28 foreclosure reconveyance that are unfair or commercially
29 unreasonable or engage in any other unfair conduct;
30 (2) represent, directly or indirectly, that the:
31 (A) foreclosure purchaser is acting:
32 (i) as an adviser or a consultant; or
33 (ii) in any other manner on behalf of the homeowner;
34 (B) foreclosure purchaser is assisting the homeowner to
35 save the residence; or
36 (C) foreclosure purchaser is assisting the homeowner in
37 preventing a foreclosure if the result of the transaction is
38 that the homeowner will not complete a redemption of the
39 property; or
40 (3) until the homeowner's right to rescind or cancel the
41 foreclosure reconveyance agreement has expired:
42 (A) record any document, including an instrument or
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

284
1 conveyance, signed by the homeowner; or
2 (B) transfer to a third party or encumber, or purport to
3 transfer to a third party or encumber, any interest in the
4 residential real property in foreclosure.
5 Sec. 6. A foreclosure purchaser shall make a detailed accounting
6 of the basis for the amount of payment made to a homeowner of
7 real property resold within eighteen (18) months after entering into
8 a foreclosure reconveyance agreement on a form prescribed by the
9 attorney general.
10 Sec. 7. A foreclosure consultant shall retain all records and
11 documents, including the foreclosure consultant contract, related
12 to services performed on behalf of a homeowner for at least three
13 (3) years after the termination or conclusion of the foreclosure
14 consultant contract entered into by the foreclosure consultant and
15 the homeowner.
16 Sec. 8. A foreclosure consultant may not represent to a
17 homeowner that the foreclosure consultant is endorsed, sponsored,
18 or affiliated with any governmental or government sponsored
19 agency or program.
20 Chapter 5. Enforcement
21 Sec. 1. A person who knowingly or intentionally violates this
22 article commits:
23 (1) a Class A misdemeanor;
24 (2) a deceptive act that is actionable by the attorney general
25 under IC 24-5-0.5-4 and is subject to the penalties and
26 remedies available to the attorney general under IC 24-5-0.5;
27 and
28 (3) a deceptive act that is actionable by the attorney general
29 under IC 37-5-8-2 and is subject to the penalties and remedies
30 available to the attorney general under IC 37-5.
31 Sec. 2. (a) A homeowner may bring an action against a person
32 for damages incurred as a result of a violation of this article.
33 (b) A homeowner who:
34 (1) brings an action under this section; and
35 (2) is awarded damages;
36 may seek reasonable attorney's fees.
37 Sec. 3. (a) A court may award attorney's fees under section 2(b)
38 of this chapter.
39 (b) If the court finds that a person willfully or knowingly
40 violated this article, the court may award damages equal to three
41 (3) times the amount of actual damages.
42 Sec. 4. (a) The Indiana housing and community development
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

285
1 authority shall maintain a list of nonprofit organizations that:
2 (1) offer counseling or advice to homeowners in foreclosure or
3 loan defaults; and
4 (2) do not contract for services with for-profit lenders or
5 foreclosure purchasers.
6 (b) The Indiana housing and community development authority
7 shall provide names and telephone numbers of the organizations
8 described in subsection (a) to a homeowner upon request.
9 Sec. 5. The attorney general may adopt rules under IC 4-22-2
10 necessary to implement this article.
11 Sec. 6. Except as provided in IC 24-5-15-7(d), this article may
12 not be construed to preempt the provisions of IC 24-5-15-1 through
13 IC 24-5-15-11.
14 ARTICLE 5. HOME LOAN PRACTICES
15 Chapter 1. Application
16 Sec. 1. Except for IC 37-5-3-7(c)(3), IC 37-5-3-7(c)(4), and
17 IC 37-5-3-7(c)(5), this article does not apply to:
18 (1) a loan made or acquired by a person organized or
19 chartered under the laws of this state, any other state, or the
20 United States relating to banks, trust companies, savings
21 associations, savings banks, credit unions, or industrial loan
22 and investment companies; or
23 (2) a loan:
24 (A) that can be purchased by the Federal National
25 Mortgage Association, the Federal Home Loan Mortgage
26 Association, or the Federal Home Loan Bank;
27 (B) to be insured by the United States Department of
28 Housing and Urban Development;
29 (C) to be guaranteed by the United States Department of
30 Veterans Affairs;
31 (D) to be made or guaranteed by the United States
32 Department of Agriculture Rural Housing Service;
33 (E) to be funded by the Indiana housing and community
34 development authority; or
35 (F) with a principal amount that exceeds the conforming
36 loan size limit for a single family dwelling as established by
37 the Federal National Mortgage Association.
38 Chapter 2. Definitions
39 Sec. 1. The definitions in this chapter apply throughout this
40 article.
41 Sec. 2. "Benchmark rate" means the interest rate established
42 under Section 152 of the Federal Home Ownership and Equity
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

286
1 Protection Act of 1994 (15 U.S.C. 1602 (aa)) and the regulations
2 adopted under that act by the Federal Reserve Board, including 12
3 CFR 226.32 and the Official Staff Commentary to the regulations
4 as amended.
5 Sec. 3. "Bona fide discount points" means loan discount points
6 that:
7 (1) are knowingly paid by the borrower;
8 (2) are paid for the express purpose of reducing the interest
9 rate applicable to the loan;
10 (3) reduce the interest rate from an interest rate that does not
11 exceed the benchmark rate; and
12 (4) are recouped within the first four (4) years of the
13 scheduled loan payments;
14 if the reduction in the interest rate that is achieved by the payment
15 of the loan discount points reduces the interest charged on the
16 scheduled payments so that the borrower's dollar amount of
17 savings in interest during the first four (4) years of the loan is equal
18 to or greater than the dollar amount of loan discount points paid
19 by the borrower.
20 Sec. 4. "Borrower" means a person obligated to repay a home
21 loan, including a coborrower, cosigner, or guarantor.
22 Sec. 5. "Bridge loan" means temporary or short term financing
23 with a maturity of less than eighteen (18) months that requires
24 payments of interest only until the entire unpaid balance is due and
25 payable.
26 Sec. 6. (a) "Creditor" means:
27 (1) a person:
28 (A) who regularly extends consumer credit that is subject
29 to a finance charge or that is payable by written agreement
30 in more than four (4) installments; and
31 (B) to whom the debt arising from a home loan transaction
32 is initially payable; or
33 (2) a person who brokers a home loan, including a person
34 who:
35 (A) directly or indirectly solicits, processes, places, or
36 negotiates home loans for others;
37 (B) offers to solicit, process, place, or negotiate home loans
38 for others; or
39 (C) closes home loans that may be in the person's own
40 name with funds provided by others and that are
41 thereafter assigned to the person providing funding for the
42 loans.
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

287
1 (b) The term does not include:
2 (1) a servicer;
3 (2) a state or local housing finance authority;
4 (3) any other state or local governmental or
5 quasi-governmental entity; or
6 (4) an attorney providing legal services in association with the
7 closing of a home loan.
8 Sec. 7. (a) "Deceptive act" means:
9 (1) an act or a practice as part of a mortgage transaction (as
10 defined in IC 37-5-3-7(a)), or of a real estate transaction (as
11 defined in IC 37-5-3-7(b)), in which a person at the time of the
12 transaction knowingly or intentionally:
13 (A) makes a material misrepresentation; or
14 (B) conceals material information regarding the terms or
15 conditions of the transaction; or
16 (2) a violation of IC 37-4 concerning mortgage rescue
17 protection fraud as set forth in IC 37-4-5-1.
18 (b) For purposes of this section, "knowingly" means having
19 actual knowledge at the time of the transaction.
20 Sec. 8. (a) "High cost home loan" means a home loan with:
21 (1) a trigger rate that exceeds the benchmark rate; or
22 (2) total points and fees that exceed:
23 (A) five percent (5%) of the loan principal for a home loan
24 having a loan principal of at least forty thousand dollars
25 ($40,000); or
26 (B) six percent (6%) of the loan principal for a home loan
27 having a loan principal of less than forty thousand dollars
28 ($40,000).
29 (b) Beginning July 1, 2006, the dollar amounts set forth in this
30 section are subject to change at the times and according to the
31 procedure set forth in the provisions of IC 37-2-1-5 concerning the
32 adjustment of dollar amounts in IC 37-2.
33 Sec. 9. (a) "Home loan" means a loan, other than an open end
34 credit plan, a reverse mortgage transaction, or a loan described in
35 IC 37-5-1-1, that is secured by a mortgage or deed of trust on real
36 estate in Indiana on which there is located or will be located a
37 structure or structures:
38 (1) designed primarily for occupancy of one (1) to four (4)
39 families; and
40 (2) that is or will be occupied by a borrower as the borrower's
41 principal dwelling.
42 (b) The term does not include a land contract.
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

288
1 Sec. 9.5. "Land contract" means a contract for the sale of real
2 estate in which the seller of the real estate retains legal title to the
3 real estate until the total contract price is paid by the buyer.
4 Sec. 10. (a) Except as provided in subsection (b), "points and
5 fees" means the total of the following:
6 (1) Points and fees (as defined in 12 CFR 226.32(b)(1) on
7 January 1, 2004).
8 (2) All compensation paid directly or indirectly to a mortgage
9 broker, including a broker that originates a loan in the
10 broker's own name.
11 As used in subdivision (2), "compensation" does not include a
12 payment included in subdivision (1).
13 (b) The term does not include the following:
14 (1) Bona fide discount points.
15 (2) An amount not to exceed one and one-half (1 1/2) points in
16 indirect broker compensation, if the terms of the loan do not
17 include:
18 (A) a prepayment penalty, in the case of a home loan
19 described in IC 37-5-3-6(b); or
20 (B) a prepayment penalty that exceeds two percent (2%) of
21 the home loan principal, in the case of a home loan other
22 than a home loan described in IC 37-5-3-6(b).
23 (3) Reasonable fees paid to an affiliate of the creditor.
24 (4) Interest prepaid by the borrower for the month in which
25 the home loan is closed.
26 Sec. 11. "Political subdivision" means a municipality, school
27 district, public library, local housing authority, fire protection
28 district, public transportation corporation, local building
29 authority, local hospital authority or corporation, local airport
30 authority, special service district, special taxing district, or any
31 other type of local governmental corporate entity.
32 Sec. 12. "Rate" means the interest rate charged on a home loan,
33 based on an annual simple interest yield.
34 Sec. 13. "Total loan amount" means the principal of the home
35 loan minus the points and fees that are included in the principal
36 amount of the loan.
37 Sec. 14. "Trigger rate" means:
38 (1) for fixed rate home loans in which the interest rate will not
39 vary during the term of the loan, the rate as of the date of
40 closing;
41 (2) for home loans in which the interest varies according to an
42 index, the sum of the index rate as of the date of closing plus
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

289
1 the maximum margin permitted at any time under the loan
2 agreement; or
3 (3) for all other home loans in which the rate may vary at any
4 time during the term of the loan, the maximum rate that may
5 be charged during the term of the home loan.
6 Chapter 3. Prohibited Lending Practices Generally
7 Sec. 0.1. A person is not subject to a prohibition or requirement
8 of:
9 (1) IC 24-9-3 (before its repeal) and IC 24-9-4 (before its
10 repeal), both as added by P.L.73-2004; or
11 (2) this chapter and IC 37-5-4;
12 with respect to a loan made before January 1, 2005.
13 Sec. 1. (a) A creditor making a home loan may not finance,
14 directly or indirectly, any:
15 (1) credit life insurance;
16 (2) credit disability insurance;
17 (3) credit unemployment insurance;
18 (4) credit property insurance; or
19 (5) payments directly or indirectly for any cancellation
20 suspension agreement or contract.
21 (b) Insurance premiums, debt cancellation fees, or suspension
22 fees calculated and paid on a monthly basis are not considered to
23 be financed by the creditor for purposes of this chapter.
24 Sec. 2. (a) A creditor may not knowingly or intentionally replace
25 or consolidate a zero (0) interest rate or other subsidized low rate
26 loan made by a governmental or nonprofit lender with a high cost
27 home loan within the first ten (10) years of the subsidized low rate
28 loan unless the current holder of the loan consents in writing to the
29 refinancing.
30 (b) For purposes of this section, a "subsidized low rate loan" is
31 a loan that carries a current interest rate of at least two (2)
32 percentage points below the current yield on treasury securities
33 with a comparable maturity. If the loan's current interest rate is
34 either a discounted introductory rate or a rate that automatically
35 steps up over time, the fully indexed rate or the fully stepped up
36 rate, as appropriate, should be used instead of the current rate to
37 determine whether a loan is a subsidized low rate loan.
38 (c) Each mortgage or deed of trust securing a zero (0) interest
39 rate or other subsidized low rate loan executed after January 1,
40 2005, must prominently display the following on the face of the
41 instrument:
42 "This instrument secures a zero (0) interest rate or other
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

290
1 subsidized low rate loan subject to IC 37-5-3-2.".
2 (d) A creditor may reasonably rely on the presence or absence
3 of the statement described in subsection (c) on the face of an
4 instrument executed after January 1, 2005, as conclusive proof of
5 the existence or nonexistence of a zero (0) interest rate or other
6 subsidized low rate loan.
7 Sec. 3. A creditor may not recommend or encourage default on
8 an existing loan or other debt before and in connection with the
9 closing or planned closing of a home loan that refinances all or part
10 of the existing loan or debt.
11 Sec. 4. A creditor shall treat each payment made by a borrower
12 in regard to a home loan as posted on the same business day as the
13 payment was received by the creditor, servicer, or creditor's agent,
14 or at the address provided to the borrower by the creditor,
15 servicer, or creditor's agent for making payments.
16 Sec. 5. (a) A home loan agreement may not contain a provision
17 that permits the creditor, in the creditor's sole discretion, to
18 accelerate the indebtedness without material cause.
19 (b) This section does not prohibit acceleration of a home loan in
20 good faith due to the borrower's failure to abide by the material
21 terms of the loan.
22 Sec. 6. (a) A creditor may not charge a fee for informing or
23 transmitting to a person the balance due to pay off a home loan or
24 to provide a written release upon prepayment. A creditor must
25 provide, in writing, a payoff balance not later than seven (7)
26 business days (excluding legal public holidays, Saturdays, and
27 Sundays) after the request is received by the creditor. A payoff
28 statement provided by a creditor under this subsection must show
29 the date the statement was prepared and itemize the unpaid
30 principal balance and each fee, charge, or other sum included
31 within the payoff amount. For purposes of this subsection, "fee"
32 does not include actual charges incurred by a creditor for express
33 or priority delivery of home loan documents to the borrower if
34 such delivery is requested by the borrower.
35 (b) This subsection applies to a home loan, or the refinancing or
36 consolidation of a home loan, that:
37 (1) is closed after June 30, 2009; and
38 (2) has an interest rate that is subject to change at one (1) or
39 more times during the term of the home loan.
40 A creditor in a transaction to which this subsection applies may not
41 contract for and may not charge the borrower a prepayment fee or
42 penalty.
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

291
1 (c) This subsection applies to a home loan with respect to which
2 any installment or minimum payment due is delinquent for at least
3 sixty (60) days. The creditor, servicer, or the creditor's agent shall
4 acknowledge a written offer made in connection with a proposed
5 short sale not later than five (5) business days (excluding legal
6 public holidays, Saturdays, and Sundays) after the date of the offer
7 if the offer complies with the requirements for a qualified written
8 request set forth in 12 U.S.C. 2605(e)(1)(B). The creditor, servicer,
9 or creditor's agent is required to acknowledge a written offer made
10 in connection with a proposed short sale from a third party acting
11 on behalf of the debtor only if the debtor has provided written
12 authorization for the creditor, servicer, or creditor's agent to do so.
13 Not later than thirty (30) business days (excluding legal public
14 holidays, Saturdays, and Sundays) after receipt of an offer under
15 this subsection, the creditor, servicer, or creditor's agent shall
16 respond to the offer with an acceptance or a rejection of the offer.
17 The thirty (30) day period described in this subsection may be
18 extended for not more than fifteen (15) business days (excluding
19 legal public holidays, Saturdays, and Sundays) if, before the end of
20 the thirty (30) day period, the creditor, the servicer, or the
21 creditor's agent notifies the debtor of the extension and the reason
22 the extension is needed. As used in this subsection, "short sale"
23 means a transaction in which the property that is the subject of a
24 home loan is sold for an amount that is less than the amount of the
25 borrower's outstanding obligation on the home loan. A creditor, a
26 servicer, or a creditor's agent that fails to respond to an offer
27 within the time prescribed by this subsection is liable in accordance
28 with 12 U.S.C. 2605(f) in any action brought under that section.
29 Sec. 7. (a) As used in this section, "mortgage transaction"
30 includes the following:
31 (1) A home loan subject to this article.
32 (2) To the extent allowed under federal law, a loan described
33 in IC 37-5-1-1 that is secured by a mortgage or a land
34 contract (or another consensual security interest equivalent
35 to a mortgage or a land contract) on real estate in Indiana on
36 which there is located or will be located a structure or
37 structures:
38 (A) designed primarily for occupancy of one (1) to four (4)
39 families; and
40 (B) that is or will be occupied by a borrower as the
41 borrower's principal dwelling.
42 (3) A first lien mortgage transaction (as defined in
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

292
1 IC 37-1-2-18) subject to IC 37-1.
2 (4) A consumer credit sale subject to IC 37-2-3 in which a
3 mortgage or a land contract (or another consensual security
4 interest equivalent to a mortgage or a land contract) that
5 constitutes a lien is created or retained against land:
6 (A) that is located in Indiana; and
7 (B) upon which there is constructed or intended to be
8 constructed a dwelling that is or will be used by the debtor
9 primarily for personal, family, or household purposes.
10 (5) A consumer loan subject to IC 37-2-4 in which a mortgage
11 or a land contract (or another consensual security interest
12 equivalent to a mortgage or a land contract) that constitutes
13 a lien is created or retained against land:
14 (A) that is located in Indiana; and
15 (B) upon which there is constructed or intended to be
16 constructed a dwelling that is or will be used by the debtor
17 primarily for personal, family, or household purposes.
18 (6) A loan in which a mortgage or a land contract (or another
19 consensual security interest equivalent to a mortgage or a
20 land contract) that constitutes a lien is created or retained
21 against land:
22 (A) that is located in Indiana;
23 (B) upon which there is constructed or intended to be
24 constructed a dwelling that is not or will not be used by the
25 borrower primarily for personal, family, or household
26 purposes; and
27 (C) that is classified as residential for property tax
28 purposes.
29 The term includes a loan that is secured by land in Indiana
30 upon which there is constructed or intended to be constructed
31 a dwelling that is purchased by or through the borrower for
32 investment or other business purposes.
33 (7) A reverse mortgage transaction that is secured by real
34 estate in Indiana on which there is located a structure that is
35 occupied by a borrower as the borrower's principal dwelling.
36 (b) As used in this section, "real estate transaction" means the
37 sale or lease of any legal or equitable interest in real estate:
38 (1) that is located in Indiana;
39 (2) upon which there is constructed or intended to be
40 constructed a dwelling; and
41 (3) that is classified as residential for property tax purposes.
42 (c) A person may not do any of the following:
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

293
1 (1) Divide a home loan transaction into separate parts with
2 the intent of evading a provision of this article.
3 (2) Structure a home loan transaction as an open-end loan
4 with the intent of evading the provisions of this article if the
5 home loan would be a high cost home loan if the home loan
6 had been structured as a closed-end loan.
7 (3) Engage in a deceptive act in connection with a mortgage
8 transaction or a real estate transaction.
9 (4) Engage in, or solicit to engage in, a real estate transaction
10 or a mortgage transaction without a permit or license
11 required by law.
12 (5) With respect to a real estate transaction or a mortgage
13 transaction, represent that:
14 (A) the transaction has:
15 (i) certain terms or conditions; or
16 (ii) the sponsorship or approval of a particular person or
17 entity;
18 that it does not have and that the person knows or
19 reasonably should know it does not have; or
20 (B) the real estate or property that is the subject of the
21 transaction has any improvements, appurtenances, uses,
22 characteristics, or associated benefits that it does not have
23 and that the person knows or reasonably should know it
24 does not have.
25 (6) Maintain or offer to maintain an account for the receipt of
26 funds for the payment of real estate taxes and insurance
27 unless the person is any of the following:
28 (A) Any of the following that is chartered under the laws
29 of a state or the United States:
30 (i) A bank.
31 (ii) A savings and loan association.
32 (iii) A credit union.
33 (iv) A savings bank.
34 (B) The creditor in a mortgage transaction.
35 (C) A mortgage servicer acting on behalf of the creditor in
36 a mortgage transaction.
37 (D) A closing agent (as defined in IC 27-7-3.7-1).
38 (7) Fail to provide the notice required under subsection (d),
39 within the time specified in subsection (d), if the person is a
40 seller in a real estate transaction described in subsection (d).
41 (d) This subsection applies to a real estate transaction that
42 involves a land contract between the seller and the buyer in the
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

294
1 transaction. If the real estate that is the subject of the transaction
2 is subject to any encumbrance, including any tax lien, foreclosure
3 action, legal judgment, or other encumbrance affecting the title to
4 the real estate, the seller must provide written notice by certified
5 mail, return receipt requested, of the encumbrance to the buyer:
6 (1) not later than the time the land contract is executed, if the
7 encumbrance is created before or at the time the land
8 contract is executed; or
9 (2) not later than ten (10) business days after the
10 encumbrance is created, if the encumbrance is created after
11 the land contract is executed.
12 Sec. 8. A person seeking to enforce section 7(c)(3), 7(c)(4), or
13 7(c)(5) of this chapter may not knowingly or intentionally
14 intimidate, coerce, or harass another person.
15 Sec. 9. It is unlawful for a creditor to discriminate against any
16 applicant with respect to any aspect of a credit transaction on the
17 basis of race, color, religion, national origin, sex, marital status, or
18 age, if the applicant has the ability to contract.
19 Chapter 4. Additional Prohibitions for High Cost Home Loans
20 Sec. 0.1. A person is not subject to a prohibition or requirement
21 of:
22 (1) IC 24-9-3 (before its repeal) and IC 24-9-4 (before its
23 repeal), both as added by P.L.73-2004; or
24 (2) this chapter and IC 37-5-3;
25 with respect to a loan made before January 1, 2005.
26 Sec. 1. The following additional limitations and prohibited
27 practices apply to a high cost home loan:
28 (1) A creditor making a high cost home loan may not directly
29 or indirectly finance any points and fees.
30 (2) This subdivision does not apply to a high cost home loan
31 described in IC 37-5-3-6(b). Prepayment fees or penalties may
32 not be included in the loan documents for a high cost home
33 loan or charged to the borrower if the fees or penalties exceed
34 in total two percent (2%) of the high cost home loan amount
35 prepaid during the first twenty-four (24) months after the
36 high cost home loan closing.
37 (3) This subdivision does not apply to a high cost home loan
38 described in IC 37-5-3-6(b). A prepayment penalty may not be
39 contracted for after the second year following the high cost
40 home loan closing.
41 (4) This subdivision does not apply to a high cost home loan
42 described in IC 37-5-3-6(b). A creditor may not include a
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

295
1 prepayment penalty fee in a high cost home loan unless the
2 creditor offers the borrower the option of choosing a loan
3 product without a prepayment fee. The terms of the offer
4 must be made in writing and must be initialed by the
5 borrower. The document containing the offer must be clearly
6 labeled in large bold type and must include the following
7 disclosure:
8 "LOAN PRODUCT CHOICE
9 I was provided with an offer to accept a product both with
10 and without a prepayment penalty provision. I have chosen
11 to accept the product with a prepayment penalty.".
12 (5) A creditor shall not sell or otherwise assign a high cost
13 home loan without furnishing the following statement to the
14 purchaser or assignee:
15 "NOTICE: This is a loan subject to special rules under
16 IC 37-5. Purchasers or assignees may be liable for all
17 claims and defenses with respect to the loan that the
18 borrower could assert against the lender.".
19 (6) A mortgage or deed of trust that secures a high cost home
20 loan at the time the mortgage or deed of trust is recorded
21 must prominently display the following on the face of the
22 instrument:
23 "This instrument secures a high cost home loan as defined
24 in IC 37-5-2-8.".
25 (7) A creditor making a high cost home loan may not finance,
26 directly or indirectly, any life or health insurance.
27 Sec. 2. A creditor may not knowingly or intentionally:
28 (1) refinance a high cost home loan by charging points and
29 fees on the part of the proceeds of the new high cost home
30 loan that is used to refinance the existing high cost loan within
31 four (4) years of the origination of the existing high cost home
32 loan; or
33 (2) divide a home loan transaction into multiple transactions
34 with the effect of evading this article. Where multiple
35 transactions are involved, the total points and fees charged in
36 all transactions shall be considered when determining
37 whether the protections of this section apply.
38 Sec. 3. Notwithstanding IC 37-2-4-15, a high cost home loan
39 agreement may not require a scheduled payment that is more than
40 twice as large as the average of earlier scheduled monthly
41 payments under the high cost home loan agreement unless the
42 payment becomes due and payable at least one hundred twenty
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

296
1 (120) months after the date of the high cost home loan. This
2 prohibition does not apply if:
3 (1) the payment schedule is adjusted to account for the
4 seasonal or irregular income of the borrower; or
5 (2) the loan is a bridge loan connected with or related to the
6 acquisition or construction of a dwelling intended to become
7 the borrower's principal dwelling.
8 Sec. 4. (a) Except as provided in subsection (b), a high cost home
9 loan may not include payment terms under which the outstanding
10 principal balance will increase at any time over the course of the
11 high cost home loan because the regular periodic payments do not
12 cover the full amount of interest due.
13 (b) This section does not apply to a temporary forbearance that
14 is requested by a borrower regarding a high cost home loan.
15 Sec. 5. A high cost home loan may not contain a provision that
16 increases the interest rate after default. However, this section does
17 not apply to interest rate changes in a variable rate loan otherwise
18 consistent with the provisions of the high cost home loan
19 documents if the change in the interest rate is not triggered by the
20 event of default or the acceleration of the indebtedness.
21 Sec. 6. A high cost home loan may not include terms under
22 which more than two (2) periodic payments required under the
23 high cost home loan are consolidated and paid in advance from the
24 high cost home loan proceeds provided to the borrower.
25 Sec. 7. A creditor may not make a high cost home loan without
26 first providing the borrower information to facilitate contact with
27 a nonprofit counseling agency certified by:
28 (1) the United States Department of Housing and Urban
29 Development; or
30 (2) the Indiana housing and community development
31 authority under IC 5-20-1-4(d);
32 at the same time as the good faith estimates are provided to the
33 borrower in accordance with the requirements of the federal Real
34 Estate Settlement Procedures Act (12 U.S.C. 2601 et seq.) as
35 amended.
36 Sec. 8. (a) A creditor may not make a high cost home loan
37 without regard to repayment ability.
38 (b) If a creditor presents evidence that the creditor followed
39 commercially reasonable practices in determining the borrower's
40 debt to income ratio, there is a rebuttable presumption that the
41 creditor made the high cost home loan with due regard to
42 repayment ability. For purposes of this section, there is a
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

297
1 rebuttable presumption that the borrower's statement of income
2 provided to the creditor is true and complete.
3 (c) Commercially reasonable practices include the use of:
4 (1) the debt to income ratio:
5 (A) listed in 38 CFR 36.4337(c)(1); and
6 (B) defined in 38 CFR 36.4337(d); and
7 (2) the residual income guidelines established under:
8 (A) 38 CFR 36.4337(e); and
9 (B) United States Department of Veterans Affairs form
10 26-6393.
11 Sec. 9. A creditor may not pay a contractor under a home
12 improvement contract from the proceeds of a high cost home loan
13 unless:
14 (1) the creditor is presented with a signed and dated
15 completion certificate showing that the home improvements
16 have been completed; and
17 (2) the instrument is payable to the borrower or jointly to the
18 borrower and the contractor or, at the election of the
19 borrower, through a third party escrow agent under a written
20 agreement signed by the borrower, the creditor, and the
21 contractor before the disbursement.
22 Sec. 10. A creditor may not charge a borrower any fees or other
23 charges to modify, renew, extend, or amend a high cost home loan
24 or to defer a payment due under the terms of a high cost home
25 loan.
26 Sec. 11. A creditor may not make a high cost home loan unless
27 the creditor has given the following notice, in writing, to the
28 borrower not later than the time that notice is required under 12
29 CFR 226.31(c):
30 "NOTICE TO BORROWER
31 YOU SHOULD BE AWARE THAT YOU MIGHT BE ABLE
32 TO OBTAIN A LOAN AT A LOWER COST. YOU SHOULD
33 COMPARE LOAN RATES, COSTS, AND FEES.
34 MORTGAGE LOAN RATES AND CLOSING COSTS AND
35 FEES VARY BASED ON MANY FACTORS, INCLUDING
36 YOUR PARTICULAR CREDIT AND FINANCIAL
37 CIRCUMSTANCES, YOUR EMPLOYMENT HISTORY,
38 THE LOAN-TO-VALUE REQUESTED, AND THE TYPE
39 OF PROPERTY THAT WILL SECURE YOUR LOAN. THE
40 LOAN RATE, COSTS, AND FEES COULD ALSO VARY
41 BASED ON WHICH CREDITOR OR BROKER YOU
42 SELECT.
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

298
1 IF YOU ACCEPT THE TERMS OF THIS LOAN, THE
2 CREDITOR WILL HAVE A MORTGAGE LIEN ON YOUR
3 HOME. YOU COULD LOSE YOUR HOME AND ANY
4 MONEY YOU HAVE PAID IF YOU DO NOT MEET YOUR
5 PAYMENT OBLIGATIONS UNDER THE LOAN.
6 YOU SHOULD CONSULT AN ATTORNEY AND A
7 QUALIFIED INDEPENDENT CREDIT COUNSELOR OR
8 OTHER EXPERIENCED FINANCIAL ADVISER
9 REGARDING THE RATE, FEES, AND PROVISIONS OF
10 THIS MORTGAGE LOAN BEFORE YOU PROCEED. A
11 LIST OF QUALIFIED COUNSELORS IS AVAILABLE
12 FROM THE INDIANA HOUSING AND COMMUNITY
13 DEVELOPMENT AUTHORITY.
14 YOU ARE NOT REQUIRED TO COMPLETE THIS LOAN
15 AGREEMENT MERELY BECAUSE YOU HAVE
16 RECEIVED THIS DISCLOSURE OR HAVE SIGNED A
17 LOAN APPLICATION. REMEMBER, PROPERTY TAXES
18 AND HOMEOWNER'S INSURANCE ARE YOUR
19 RESPONSIBILITY. NOT ALL CREDITORS PROVIDE
20 ESCROW SERVICES FOR THESE PAYMENTS. YOU
21 SHOULD ASK YOUR CREDITOR ABOUT THESE
22 SERVICES.
23 ALSO, YOUR PAYMENTS ON EXISTING DEBTS
24 CONTRIBUTE TO YOUR CREDIT RATINGS. YOU
25 SHOULD NOT ACCEPT ANY ADVICE TO IGNORE
26 YOUR REGULAR PAYMENTS TO YOUR EXISTING
27 CREDITORS.".
28 Sec. 12. Without regard to whether a borrower is acting
29 individually or on behalf of others similarly situated, a provision
30 of a high cost home loan agreement that:
31 (1) requires arbitration of a claim or defense;
32 (2) allows a party to require a borrower to assert a claim or
33 defense in a forum that is:
34 (A) less convenient;
35 (B) more costly; or
36 (C) more dilatory;
37 for the resolution of the dispute than an Indiana court in
38 which the borrower may otherwise bring a claim or defense;
39 or
40 (3) limits in any way any claim or defense the borrower may
41 have;
42 is unconscionable and void.
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

299
1 Chapter 5. Claims, Defenses, Remedies
2 Sec. 1. (a) A person who purchases or is otherwise assigned a
3 high cost home loan is subject to all affirmative claims and any
4 defenses, except for an affirmative claim or defense pursuant to
5 IC 37-5-3-7, with respect to the high cost home loan that the
6 borrower could assert against a creditor or broker of the high cost
7 home loan. However, this section does not apply if the purchaser or
8 assignee demonstrates by a preponderance of the evidence that a
9 reasonable person exercising ordinary due diligence could not
10 determine that the loan was a high cost home loan. A purchaser or
11 an assignee is presumed to have exercised reasonable due diligence
12 if the purchaser or assignee:
13 (1) has in place at the time of the purchase or assignment of
14 the subject loans, policies that expressly prohibit the purchase
15 or acceptance of the assignment of any high cost home loans;
16 (2) requires by contract that a seller or an assignor of home
17 loans to the purchaser or assignee represents and warrants to
18 the purchaser or assignee that either:
19 (A) the seller or assignor will not sell or reassign any high
20 cost home loans to the purchaser or assignee; or
21 (B) the seller or assignor is a beneficiary of a
22 representation and warranty from a previous seller or
23 assignor to that effect;
24 (3) exercises reasonable due diligence:
25 (A) at the time of purchase or assignment of home loans;
26 or
27 (B) within a reasonable period after the purchase or
28 assignment of home loans;
29 intended by the purchaser or assignee to prevent the
30 purchaser or assignee from purchasing or taking assignment
31 of any high cost home loans; or
32 (4) satisfies the requirements of subdivisions (1) and (2) and
33 establishes that a reasonable person exercising ordinary due
34 diligence could not determine that the loan was a high cost
35 home loan based on the:
36 (A) documentation required by the federal Truth in
37 Lending Act (15 U.S.C. 1601 et seq.); and
38 (B) itemization of the amount financed and other
39 disbursement disclosures.
40 (b) A borrower acting only in an individual capacity may assert
41 against the creditor or any subsequent holder or assignee of a high
42 cost home loan:
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

300
1 (1) a violation of IC 37-5-4-2 as a defense, claim, or
2 counterclaim, after:
3 (A) an action to enjoin foreclosure or to preserve or obtain
4 possession of the dwelling that secures the loan is initiated;
5 (B) an action to collect on the loan or foreclose on the
6 collateral securing the loan is initiated; or
7 (C) the loan is more than sixty (60) days in default;
8 within three (3) years after the closing of a home loan;
9 (2) a violation of this article in connection to the high cost
10 home loan as a defense, claim, or counterclaim in an original
11 action within five (5) years after the closing of a high cost
12 home loan; and
13 (3) any defense, claim, counterclaim, or action to enjoin
14 foreclosure or preserve or obtain possession of the home that
15 secures the loan, including a violation of this article after:
16 (A) an action to collect on the loan or foreclose on the
17 collateral securing the loan is initiated;
18 (B) the debt arising from the loan is accelerated; or
19 (C) the loan is more than sixty (60) days in default;
20 at any time during the term of a high cost home loan.
21 (c) In an action, a claim, or a counterclaim brought under
22 subsection (b), the borrower may recover only amounts required
23 to reduce or extinguish the borrower's liability under a home loan
24 plus amounts required to recover costs, including reasonable
25 attorney's fees.
26 (d) The provisions of this section are effective notwithstanding
27 any other provision of law. This section shall not be construed to
28 limit the substantive rights, remedies, or procedural rights
29 available to a borrower against any creditor, assignee, or holder
30 under any other law. The rights conferred on borrowers by
31 subsections (a) and (b) are independent of each other and do not
32 limit each other.
33 Sec. 2. (a) If a creditor asserts that grounds for acceleration
34 under the terms of a high cost home loan exist and requires the
35 payment in full of all sums secured by the security instrument, the
36 borrower or a person authorized to act on the borrower's behalf at
37 any time before the title is transferred by means of foreclosure,
38 judicial proceeding and sale, or otherwise may cure the default and
39 reinstate the high cost home loan by tendering the amount or
40 performance as specified in the security instrument.
41 (b) If the borrower cures the default on a high cost home loan,
42 the original loan terms shall be reinstated, and any acceleration of
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

301
1 any obligation under the security instrument or note arising from
2 the default is nullified as of the date of the cure.
3 Sec. 3. (a) A creditor making a high cost home loan that has the
4 right to foreclose must use the judicial foreclosure procedures of
5 the state in which the property securing the high cost home loan is
6 located. The borrower has the right to assert in the proceeding the
7 nonexistence of a default and any other claim or defense to
8 acceleration and foreclosure, including any claim or defense based
9 on any violations of this article.
10 (b) This section is not intended and shall not be construed to
11 allow any claim or defense otherwise barred by any statute of
12 limitation or repose.
13 Sec. 4. (a) This section does not apply to a violation of
14 IC 37-5-3-7(c)(4), IC 37-5-3-7(c)(5), or IC 37-5-3-7(c)(6). A person
15 who violates this article is liable to a person who is a party to the
16 home loan transaction, mortgage transaction (as defined in
17 IC 37-5-3-7(a)), or real estate transaction (as defined in
18 IC 37-5-3-7(b)), as appropriate, that gave rise to the violation for
19 the following:
20 (1) Actual damages, including consequential damages. A
21 person is not required to demonstrate reliance in order to
22 receive actual damages.
23 (2) Statutory damages equal to two (2) times the finance
24 charges agreed to in a home loan agreement.
25 (3) Costs and reasonable attorney's fees.
26 (b) A person may be granted injunctive, declaratory, and other
27 equitable relief as the court determines appropriate in an action to
28 enforce compliance with this chapter.
29 (c) The right of rescission granted under 15 U.S.C. 1601 et seq.
30 for a violation of the federal Truth in Lending Act (15 U.S.C. 1601
31 et seq.) is available to a person acting only in an individual capacity
32 by way of recoupment as a defense against a party foreclosing on
33 a home loan at any time during the term of the loan. Any
34 recoupment claim asserted under this provision is limited to the
35 amount required to reduce or extinguish the person's liability
36 under the home loan plus amounts required to recover costs,
37 including reasonable attorney's fees. This article shall not be
38 construed to limit the recoupment rights available to a person
39 under any other law.
40 (d) The remedies provided in this section are cumulative but are
41 not intended to be the exclusive remedies available to a person.
42 Except as provided in subsection (e), a person is not required to
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

302
1 exhaust any administrative remedies under this article or under
2 any other applicable law.
3 (e) Before bringing an action regarding an alleged deceptive act
4 under this chapter, a person must:
5 (1) notify the homeowner protection unit established by
6 IC 4-6-12-2 of the alleged violation giving rise to the action;
7 and
8 (2) allow the homeowner protection unit at least ninety (90)
9 days to institute appropriate administrative and civil action
10 to redress a violation.
11 (f) An action under this chapter must be brought within five (5)
12 years after the date that the person knew, or by the exercise of
13 reasonable diligence should have known, of the violation of this
14 article.
15 (g) An award of damages under subsection (a) has priority over
16 a civil penalty imposed under this article.
17 Sec. 5. (a) If the creditor or an assignee establishes by a
18 preponderance of evidence that a violation of this article is
19 unintentional or the result of a bona fide error of law or fact
20 notwithstanding the maintenance of procedures reasonably
21 adopted to avoid any such violation or error, the validity of the
22 transaction is not affected, and no liability is imposed under section
23 4 of this chapter except in the case of a refusal to make a refund.
24 (b) Except as provided in subsection (c), a creditor in a high cost
25 home loan who in good faith fails to comply with this article is not
26 considered to have violated this article if the creditor does the
27 following before receiving notice of the failure from the borrower:
28 (1) Not later than ninety (90) days after the date of the loan
29 closing:
30 (A) makes appropriate restitution to the borrower of any
31 amounts collected in error; and
32 (B) takes necessary action to make all appropriate
33 adjustments to the loan to correct the error.
34 (2) Not later than one hundred twenty (120) days after the
35 date of the loan closing, notifies the borrower of:
36 (A) the error; and
37 (B) the amount of the required restitution or adjustment.
38 (c) Subsection (b) does not apply unless the creditor establishes
39 that the compliance failure was not intentional and resulted from
40 a bona fide error of fact or law, notwithstanding the maintenance
41 of procedures reasonably adopted to avoid the errors.
42 Sec. 6. The rights conferred by this article are in addition to
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

303
1 rights granted under any other law.
2 Chapter 6. Reporting Requirements
3 Sec. 1. (a) A servicer of a high cost home loan shall report at
4 least once each calendar quarter to a nationally recognized
5 consumer credit reporting agency both the favorable and
6 unfavorable payment history information of the borrower on
7 payments due to the creditor on a high cost home loan.
8 (b) This section does not prohibit a servicer from agreeing with
9 the borrower not to report specified payment history information
10 in the event of a resolved or an unresolved dispute with a borrower
11 and does not apply to high cost home loans held or serviced by a
12 lender for less than ninety (90) days.
13 Chapter 7. State Power to Regulate Lending
14 Sec. 1. The state is the sole regulator of the business of
15 originating, granting, servicing, and collecting loans and other
16 forms of credit in Indiana and the manner in which the business is
17 conducted. This regulation preempts all other regulation of these
18 activities by any political subdivision.
19 Sec. 2. Political subdivisions may not:
20 (1) enact, issue, or enforce ordinances, resolutions,
21 regulations, orders, requests for proposals, or requests for
22 bids pertaining to financial or lending activities, including
23 ordinances, resolutions, and rules that disqualify persons
24 from doing business with a municipality and that are based
25 upon lending terms or practices; or
26 (2) impose reporting requirements or any other obligations
27 upon persons regarding financial services or lending practices
28 or upon subsidiaries or affiliates that:
29 (A) are subject to the jurisdiction of the department of
30 financial institutions;
31 (B) are subject to the jurisdiction or regulatory supervision
32 of the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System,
33 the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency, the National
34 Credit Union Administration, the Federal Deposit
35 Insurance Corporation, the Federal Trade Commission, or
36 the United States Department of Housing and Urban
37 Development;
38 (C) are chartered by the United States Congress to engage
39 in secondary market mortgage transactions;
40 (D) are created by the Indiana housing and community
41 development authority; or
42 (E) originate, purchase, sell, assign, securitize, or service
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

304
1 property interests or obligations created by financial
2 transactions or loans made, executed, originated, or
3 purchased by persons referred to in clause (A), (B), (C), or
4 (D).
5 Chapter 8. Penalties and Enforcement
6 Sec. 1. A person who knowingly or intentionally violates this
7 article commits:
8 (1) a Class A misdemeanor; and
9 (2) except for a violation of IC 37-5-3-7(c)(4) by a person
10 required to be licensed by the department of financial
11 institutions, an act that is actionable by the attorney general
12 under IC 24-5-0.5 and is subject to the penalties listed in
13 IC 24-5-0.5.
14 Sec. 2. (a) The attorney general and the attorney general's
15 homeowner protection unit established under IC 4-6-12 shall
16 enforce this article. An action may not be brought under this
17 article more than five (5) years after the occurrence of the
18 violation.
19 (b) The attorney general may refer a matter under section 1 of
20 this chapter to a prosecuting attorney for enforcement.
21 Sec. 3. (a) This section does not apply to a violation of
22 IC 37-5-3-7(c)(4) by a person required to be licensed by the
23 department of financial institutions. The attorney general may
24 bring an action to enjoin a violation of this article. A court in which
25 the action is brought may:
26 (1) issue an injunction;
27 (2) order a person to make restitution;
28 (3) order a person to reimburse the state for reasonable costs
29 of the attorney general's investigation and prosecution of the
30 violation of this article; and
31 (4) impose a civil penalty of not more than ten thousand
32 dollars ($10,000) per violation.
33 (b) A person who violates an injunction under this section is
34 subject to a civil penalty of not more than ten thousand dollars
35 ($10,000) per violation.
36 (c) The court that issues an injunction retains jurisdiction over
37 a proceeding seeking the imposition of a civil penalty under this
38 section.
39 Sec. 4. The attorney general may file complaints with any of the
40 agencies listed in IC 4-6-12-4 to implement this chapter.
41 Chapter 9. Fees
42 Sec. 1. The county recorder shall assess a fee for each mortgage
2026 IN 169—LS 6567/DI 92

305
1 recorded that shall be paid to the county treasurer at the end of
2 each calendar month as set forth in IC 36-2-7-10(c)(2).
3 Sec. 2. On or before June 20 and December 20 of each year,
4 after completing an audit of the county treasurer's monthly reports
5 required by IC 36-2-10-16, the county auditor shall distribute to
6 the state comptroller two dollars and fifty cents ($2.50) of the
7 mortgage recording fee collected under IC 36-2-7-10(c)(2) for each
8 mortgage recorded by the county recorder. The state comptroller
9 shall deposit the money in the state general fund to be distributed
10 as described in section 3 of this chapter.
11 Sec. 3. On or before June 30 and December 31 of each year the
12 state comptroller shall distribute one dollar and twenty-five cents
13 ($1.25) of the mortgage recording fee to the state general fund and
14 one dollar and twenty-five cents ($1.25) of the mortgage recording
15 fee to the homeowner protection unit account established by
16 IC 4-6-12-9.
